AST-12.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of ePHI-containing devices and media that are disposed of according to the documented disposal policy (approved sanitization or destruction method) and maintain traceable disposal records (device identifier, disposal method, personnel, timestamp, and certificate of destruction). Also measure training coverage for personnel responsible for disposal and the number of incidents attributable to improper disposal.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (policy and required procedures) where auditors want coverage and compliance rates (percentage of disposals compliant). Counts and trends show volume and change over time; a compliance score can aggregate multiple checklist items (log completeness, method used, certificate present).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percent of disposal events for assets marked as containing ePHI that include a disposal log entry with device/asset ID, serial or asset tag, asset ePHI flag, approved sanitization/destruction method (e.g., shredding, degaussing, wiping), timestamp, personnel identifier, and supporting certificate of destruction or sanitization report; count of disposal events flagged non-compliant; training completion records for personnel authorized to perform disposals; number of incidents/breaches linked to disposal.
data_signals: asset inventory entries with ePHI flag and asset tag/serial
disposal_log entries (device ID, timestamp, personnel ID, disposal_method)
certificate_of_destruction / sanitization_report documents linked to disposal_log entry
disposal_audit_reports and audit findings
training_completion_records for disposal procedures (course id, user, completion date)
incident/violation records referencing improper disposal or media breach
asset_return/termination checklists showing device return status
cross-checks showing asset removed from active inventory after disposal
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated disposal logs tied to canonical asset inventory with device ID, method, timestamp and linked certificate_of_destruction; chain-of-custody records and regular audit reports showing 100% mapping between disposed assets and inventory entries.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off scanned receipts without asset IDs or timestamps, annual-only attestations, or missing sanitization proof.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires documented policies, approved sanitization/destruction methods and preventive processes (identification, approved methods, training) though it includes audit monitoring; primary intent is prevention of improper disposal.

AST-12.differentiation_note: This control is specifically focused on secure sanitization/destruction and chain-of-custody for ePHI-bearing devices and media (disposal logs, certificates), rather than general asset identification or return processes; its unique measurement need is verifying sanitization method evidence and complete disposal traceability.

AST-13.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of devices and media that contain ePHI which have complete lifecycle movement records (inventory entry, every transfer with actor/timestamp/purpose, and documented sanitization/disposal) and report counts of missing, overdue, or unauthorized movements and disposals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is primarily preventive (requires coverage of all devices/media), so a coverage percentage (e.g., percent of ePHI-bearing assets with complete movement & disposal records) is most useful; counts of missing records, trends over time, and rates of unauthorized movements supplement coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count devices/media flagged as containing ePHI and verify each has: (a) an inventory record (asset_id, asset_type, ePHI_flag, owner), (b) movement log entries for every transfer including device_id, actor_id, origin/destination, date/time and purpose, and (c) disposal/sanitization proof (wipe certificate or sanitization method and timestamp). Measure percent coverage, counts of missing/overdue records, and number of incidents linked to device movement.
data_signals: device inventory records (asset_id, asset_type, ePHI_flag, owner/department)
movement log entries with device_id, timestamp (date and time), actor_id (who accessed/transferred), origin and destination, and stated purpose
MDM telemetry: enrollment status, last_checkin timestamp, device location/last_seen
disposal/sanitization certificates or wipe logs (method, timestamp, verification signature)
chain-of-custody forms or transfer approval records
termination/return records showing equipment returned or marked missing
audit logs of inventory edits (who, when, what changed)
incident reports linking breaches or unauthorized access to device/media movement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated MDM and inventory logs showing device_id + timestamp + actor + purpose for every transfer, paired with cryptographic/verified sanitization records and chain-of-custody entries.
Strong: end-to-end trace for lifecycle events (acquisition → transfers → sanitization/disposal) for 100% of devices flagged as ePHI-bearing.
Weak: periodic manual spreadsheets, ad-hoc emails, or annual spot-checks without timestamps or verifiable sanitization proof; these provide limited assurance of ongoing control operation.
Weak: movement entries missing actor IDs, timestamps, or disposal proof; large counts of devices with no recent MDM check-in.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policies, an inventory management system that logs movement, and sanitization to prevent unauthorized access — indicating a preventive control focused on ensuring coverage and correct handling.

AST-13.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on end-to-end lifecycle recording and sanitization proof for ePHI-bearing devices/media (inventory + movement + disposal), unlike sibling controls that may address only access, remote configuration, or incident detection; measurement must verify chain-of-custody and sanitization artifacts rather than just detection events.

AST-14.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of electronic media items containing PHI that are sanitized or destroyed prior to re-use or disposal, track time-to-sanitization after decommissioning, and report the number of non-compliance incidents and audit findings related to media handling.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and requires demonstrating coverage (e.g., percent of media sanitized before reuse) plus counts of sanitized/destroyed items and rates/trends of non-compliance and remediation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of decommissioned electronic media items (by asset id and media type) and how many have an associated sanitization/destruction record (timestamp, sanitization method, operator); time between decommissioning and sanitization/destruction; number of media items missing sanitization evidence; audit findings and training completion for staff authorized to handle media.
data_signals: media inventory records with asset id, media type, owner, and decommissioning timestamp
sanitization/destruction event logs (asset id, timestamp, sanitization method used, operator/technician id)
destruction certificates or disposal receipts and retained audit-trail documentation
sanitization method referenced to NIST SP 800-88 or organizational policy
chain-of-custody or transfer logs showing handlers and access control actions for media
periodic audit reports referencing sampled media items and compliance findings
training completion records for personnel authorized to sanitize/dispose media
non-compliance incident records detailing breaches or mishandling involving media
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated sanitization/destruction event logs tied to asset ids, signed destruction certificates, and reconciled inventory demonstrating 100% coverage.
Strong: audit reports sampling evidence across media types with linked sanitization records and NIST SP 800-88 method mapping.
Weak: single-point attestations or one-off spreadsheets without timestamps, missing operator identity, or without reconciliation to the master inventory.
Weak: annual-only statements of policy review without continuous or periodic verification of sanitization events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes procedures to ensure media are sanitized or destroyed before re-use/disposal (preventing unauthorized disclosure), e.g., 'securely sanitizing or destroying media before re-use or disposal.'

AST-14.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires tracking sanitization/destruction events, associated methods, and inventory reconciliation for PHI-bearing media — unlike broader asset-identification or training controls, measurement must focus on end-of-life sanitization evidence and coverage.

AST-15.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of ePHI-bearing media that are disposed with verifiable destruction methods and chain-of-custody evidence, track the count of disposal events by method/vendor, and monitor employee training and audit findings related to media disposal to demonstrate policy adherence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/coverage percentage (e.g., percent of media rendered irretrievable). Counts of disposal events, rates of overdue disposals, and trending of compliance over time are useful secondary metrics to show volume, timeliness, and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track each disposed media asset record including asset identifier, ePHI classification flag, disposal method used (e.g., degauss, shred), disposal timestamp, personnel or vendor performing disposal, and existence of a destruction/certificate-of-destruction document. Also measure employee training completion rates on media handling and results from periodic disposal audits and any disposal-related incidents.
data_signals: media disposal records with asset ID, disposal timestamp, and disposal method
asset inventory entries indicating ePHI-bearing media (asset ID, owner, classification)
certificate of destruction / vendor destruction report (including vendor name and certificate ID)
chain-of-custody logs for media transfers and custody changes
LMS training completion records for media-handling training (employee ID, completion date)
audit reports of media disposal processes and exceptions found
incidents/breach records referencing improper disposal (incident ID, root cause)
terminated-employee equipment return logs (timestamps, items returned/missing)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated disposal records from asset management with immutable timestamps, matching certificate-of-destruction PDFs that include asset serial numbers and vendor NAID/certification identifiers, and continuous chain-of-custody logs.
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheets or unsigned receipts, annual attestations without per-asset destruction evidence, or training acknowledgement without LMS timestamped completion.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates ensuring media are rendered irretrievable before disposal and documents procedures to prevent unauthorized access, which is characteristic of a preventive control.

AST-15.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on evidence of secure destruction and chain-of-custody for ePHI-bearing media (disposal certificates, per-asset destruction records, and training) rather than broader asset inventory or endpoint configuration controls.

BCD-12.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of applications and datasets that have an assigned criticality tier and PHI flag in the centralized classification repository, the count of items re-evaluated within the policy-defined period, and the percentage of PHI-containing systems mapped to required security controls per policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires demonstrating coverage and policy adherence (coverage % of classified assets and % mapped to controls). Counts (number assessed, overdue) and trending (re‑evaluation cadence over time) are useful secondary measures to show ongoing maintenance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of applications and datasets with an assigned classification label and PHI flag stored in the centralized repository; count of classification assessments completed in the last 12 months; percentage of PHI-flagged systems that have documented mapping to required security measures per the classification policy; timestamps of last re-evaluation and policy version referenced.
data_signals: application inventory records with unique ID, assigned criticality tier, and classification label
dataset registry entries with PHI flag / Protected Health Information indicator
classification assessment records (assessment_id, assessor, timestamp, assessment_result)
centralized repository record last_updated timestamp and record source
mapping table of security_control_id -> criticality_tier (controls applied per classification)
policy document metadata (policy_id, version, approval_date) and referenced classification criteria
re-evaluation schedule entries and completed_review timestamps per asset/dataset
training completion logs for staff assigned to classification responsibilities (user_id, course_id, completion_date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated centralized repository with timestamps showing current classification coverage % and last re-evaluation dates; documented mapping of each PHI-flagged asset to specific security_control_ids and policy version.
Strong: training records linking named personnel to classification tasks and up-to-date assessments.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or single point-in-time reports without timestamps or evidence of periodic re-evaluation.
Weak: missing mapping between PHI-flagged systems and applied security measures or absent policy versioning.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing a formal policy, classifying assets, and applying security measures (preventive activities described as 'establish a formal policy' and 'implement tailored security controls').

BCD-12.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on classification and PHI-mapping (coverage and policy adherence) rather than operational incident response or technical remediation metrics; measurement must emphasize classification coverage, PHI identification, and mapping to controls rather than detection counts or patch timelines.

BCD-13.measurement_goal: Measure backup coverage and recoverability for systems that store ePHI (percentage of assets with retrievable backups), the frequency and success rate of backup jobs, the time to restore data during disaster recovery tests, and the percentage/count of personnel trained on the contingency plan and emergency procedures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control emphasizes coverage and compliance (e.g., retrievable copies, personnel trained, regular testing) which are best expressed as percentages (coverage/completion). Counts (backup jobs, tests) and trends (restore time over time) are useful secondary views to show volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count successful backup executions and failures per asset and per backup policy period; record restore operations with start/end timestamps and success verification for each DR/test event; track DR test frequency and measured restore durations; maintain a roster of personnel with training completion timestamps and versions of the contingency plan and assigned roles. All measurements should be linkable to asset identifiers and policy thresholds (e.g., backup frequency, retention, required test cadence).
data_signals: backup job execution timestamps and status codes (success/failure) per asset
inventory of systems storing ePHI with backup configuration and last backup timestamp
restore operation logs with start/end timestamps and verification (restore success/failure)
disaster recovery test reports with measured restore time (time-to-restore) and pass/fail results
backup retention and encryption configuration records
training attendance/completion records with personnel IDs and completion dates for contingency procedures
documented roles and responsibilities versions and assigned approvers
audit logs of backup integrity checks and backup restoration verification steps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated backup logs and restore verification records (continuous machine-generated) provide strong evidence.
Documented, repeatable DR test reports with measured restore times and pass/fail outcomes (regularly occurring) are strong; a single annual manual test without logs is weak evidence.
Training completion records tied to personnel IDs and dated sign-offs are strong; undocumented or anecdotal training statements are weak.
Complete mapping between asset inventory and backup configuration (no unscanned assets) strengthens evidence; gaps between inventory and backup records weaken it.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing plans, backup processes and training to prevent data unavailability and unauthorized loss (phrases: 'establish a comprehensive contingency plan', 'create and maintain retrievable copies', 'regular testing').

BCD-13.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on backup/recovery coverage, recoverability testing and staff preparedness for ePHI availability, rather than on access controls or logging — measurement must emphasize backup/restore execution and DR test metrics plus training records.

BCD-14.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of the contingency plan by: (1) percent of critical ePHI systems with retrievable backups meeting defined RPO/RTO targets, (2) mean time to restore operations (MTTR) vs. recovery time objectives (RTO), and (3) success rate and frequency of disaster recovery and emergency-mode operation tests plus staff training completion rates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires measuring coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of systems with retrievable backups, percent of tests passed, percent of staff trained) so percentage metrics best express coverage and SLA adherence; counts and rates (test counts, restore rates, MTTR trend) are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics an auditor would expect: (a) percentage of critical ePHI-bearing systems with successful backup snapshots within required RPO windows and last-tested restore timestamps; (b) MTTR and measured time-to-restore from recent DR exercises compared against defined RTOs; (c) disaster recovery test records showing pass/fail, restore completeness, and time-to-recovery; (d) plan version history and date of last review and number/percentage of staff who completed contingency plan training.
data_signals: backup execution timestamps per system and backup retention records
backup recovery/restore test results (pass/fail) with timestamps and restoration completeness indicators
measured time-to-restore (seconds/minutes/hours) from DR exercise logs and failover runbooks
defined RTO/RPO targets per system (policy thresholds)
disaster recovery plan revision history and last review date
frequency and results of emergency mode operation tests and tabletop exercise reports
incident communications logs (stakeholder notification timestamps) during tests
training completion records and attendance logs tied to contingency plan exercises
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated backup and restore logs showing successful end-to-end restores of ePHI within RTO/RPO during full-scale DR tests and dated plan revisions with documented corrective actions.
Moderate: recent tabletop exercise outcomes and staff training completion records without full live-restore evidence.
Weak: annual plan documents or one-off manual backup snapshots without attached restore/test logs or no measurable restore times.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires developing and documenting procedures to prepare for and prevent unacceptable downtime or data loss (phrases like 'prepare for, respond to, and recover' and 'data backup plan') which aligns primarily with preventive controls.

BCD-14.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on recovery readiness and measurable recovery outcomes (RTO/RPO, restore success rates, DR test frequency, training completion) rather than detection or access controls — measurement must emphasize restore/test evidence and SLA adherence.

BCD-15.measurement_goal: Measure the rate of contingency-plan tests executed on schedule and the effectiveness of those tests: percent of critical ePHI systems restored within policy-defined recovery time, data-recovery success rate during tests, percent of backups passing integrity checks, and the count and timeliness of documented plan revisions following tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires measurement of test execution and recovery performance (e.g., test completion rates and recovery success rates). 'rate' captures frequency/compliance of tests while 'percentage' and 'count' quantify success, coverage and revisions; trending shows improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of contingency-plan exercises (tabletop, simulation, full recovery) executed in the assessment period and their pass/fail outcomes; median and percentile restore durations per critical ePHI system (restore_start and restore_complete timestamps) compared to policy recovery time; percentage of backup integrity checks passing per backup job; number and timestamped records of plan revisions created as a direct result of tests.
data_signals: exercise execution logs with timestamp, type (tabletop/simulation/full restore), and participants/roles
test result records indicating success/failure and detailed recovery steps
system restore timestamps and duration per system (restore_start, restore_complete)
backup integrity check logs (checksum/verification result, last successful verification timestamp)
list of critical ePHI systems with assigned recovery time objective (RTO) or policy recovery thresholds
documented plan revision records with revision date, author, and description of changes
staff training / drill attendance and role performance records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated exercise execution logs and restore timestamps showing repeated successful full restores within policy RTOs, backup integrity checks with cryptographic verification, and documented plan revisions linked to test findings.
Weak: single annual tabletop report without timestamps or measurable restore results, manual summary notes with no backup verification logs, or undocumented ad-hoc drills.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on 'testing' and 'exercises' (tabletop, simulations) which are detective activities that identify weaknesses and readiness gaps.

BCD-15.differentiation_note: This control focuses on measuring testing frequency, recovery effectiveness and documented revisions (evidence of iterative improvement), rather than simply measuring backup existence or configuration — it requires test outcomes, restore durations and revision records as unique measurement needs.

BCD-16.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical ePHI assets covered by automated, encrypted backups that meet retention policy and off-site replication requirements, plus the backup job success rate and restore-test success rate against organizational policy thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., percent of critical assets backed up and encrypted). Secondary rate/count metrics capture backup frequency, job success/failure counts and restore-test success rates; trending shows ongoing reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Proportion of critical data assets (by asset ID or classification) with at least one successful encrypted backup within the required backup window and replicated to off-site/HIPAA-compliant storage; backup job success rate (successful runs/total runs) over the reporting period; number and success rate of scheduled restore tests and time-to-restore during tests compared to retention/restore policy.
data_signals: backup job execution timestamps and job IDs
backup job completion status (success/failure) and error codes
backup destination metadata (on-site | off-site | cloud provider) and provider name
encryption-at-rest flag or encryption key ID associated with each backup
asset inventory mapping showing asset ID, classification (ePHI), and inclusion in backup jobs
restore test execution records with timestamps, success/failure, and restore duration
backup retention policy application timestamp and retention expiration metadata
backup activity logs / audit entries showing configuration changes and remediation tickets for failed backups
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated backup logs with per-job success/failure, encryption metadata, off-site replication receipts, and periodic documented restore-test results that show successful recovery within policy windows.
Strong: asset-to-backup mapping proving 100% coverage of critical ePHI assets and documented retention configuration aligned to HIPAA policy.
Weak: ad-hoc/manual backup screenshots, single annual restore test without periodic tests, or backups lacking encryption/off-site replication metadata.
Weak: undocumented or inconsistent backup logs, or manual notes without audit trail and timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing regular, automated backups, encryption, retention and off-site storage to prevent data loss, which is preventative by design.

BCD-16.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on technical backup coverage, encryption, retention and restore-test evidence (operational data signals) rather than broader BC/DR governance or incident-response procedures, so measurements must map assets to backup jobs and restore-test results.

BCD-17.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of scheduled automated backups that complete successfully for all critical datasets, track restore-test success rate and time-to-restore (RTO) during recovery exercises, and verify encryption-in-transit and at-rest for backup media plus offsite/cloud HIPAA-compliant storage coverage and restricted access to backup management.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes coverage and success rates (coverage/compliance) which map to percentage metrics (e.g., backup success rate, restoration success rate). Counts (number of failed backups, restore tests) and trends (failure rate over time, RTO trending) are useful secondary measures to show frequency and direction.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute per-period (daily/weekly) backup success rate per critical asset/data set (successful backups / scheduled backups), count of restore tests executed and their pass/fail outcomes, median and 95th-percentile restore time from backup during tests, encryption status flags for backups (in-transit and at-rest), and the inventory of backup storage locations with HIPAA-compliance attestation and access-control assignments.
data_signals: backup job execution logs with timestamps and status codes (success/failure)
backup schedule records mapping critical datasets to frequency (daily/weekly) and retention policy
restore test execution records with timestamps, restored dataset identifiers, and restore duration (RTO)
backup encryption configuration flags and KMS/key-management audit entries (in-transit and at-rest)
backup storage metadata showing location (offsite/cloud provider) and compliance attestation/BAA
access control lists/role assignments for backup administrators and privileged backup management actions
failed backup error messages and root-cause/failure reason codes
backup retention and versioning records (number of versions retained per dataset)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated backup logs with per-job success/failure status and timestamps; frequent (e.g., quarterly or more frequent) documented restore tests with pass/fail results and measured RTO within policy thresholds; encryption configuration and KMS audit trails proving keys used for backups; documented offsite/cloud provider BAA and storage location metadata.
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots or ad-hoc manual backups, only annual restore test without measurable RTO, undocumented backup schedules, or missing encryption and storage attestation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description calls this a 'preventive control' and focuses on establishing systematic automated backups, encryption, and secure offsite storage to prevent data loss and breaches.

BCD-17.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of backup execution, encryption status, offsite/cloud compliance, and restore-test RTOs — unlike sibling controls that might focus on access reviews or incident response, it uniquely demands coverage and recoverability metrics tied to retention policy and storage attestation.

BCD-18.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of critical systems and PHI-bearing data covered by a current DRP, the DR test pass-rate and number of tests executed per period, and measured recovery times (actual RTO) and data loss (actual RPO) versus the documented RTOs/RPOs; also measure staff training/simulation completion rates and plan update frequency.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of critical systems with documented RTO/RPO and percent of personnel completing DR training), while counts of tests and trending of measured recovery times provide supporting evidence of operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percent of critical assets identified in the BIA that have a documented DR recovery strategy and assigned RTO/RPO. 2) Number of DR tests/simulations executed per period and the pass-rate (tests meeting success criteria). 3) Measured recovery time (start-to-recovered) and recovery point (data loss window) per test per system compared to the documented RTO/RPO; include overdue/exceeded events and incident records where RTO/RPO were violated. 4) Frequency of DR plan updates and percent of required staff with completed DR training/simulations.
data_signals: business impact analysis (BIA) asset and criticality list
documented RTO and RPO values per system/asset
DRP document versioning and last-updated timestamp
DR test execution logs with start/end timestamps and step-level timestamps
DR test results / pass-fail outcomes and test playbooks used
measured recovery time (actual RTO) per test and measured recovery point (actual RPO) per test
backup/snapshot timestamp logs and backup success/failure records
incident/outage logs showing downtime duration and cause (e.g., disaster event)
training completion records and simulation attendance rosters
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated DR test logs with timestamps and measured recovery/RPO metrics correlated to specific assets and BIA criticality; documented after-action reports and remediation tracking; recurring scheduled tests with passing outcomes.
Moderate: regular tabletop exercise reports and staff attendance records without measured recovery timings.
Weak: one-off manual attestations or outdated DR plan documents without test results, timestamps, or measured RTO/RPO comparisons.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly labels it 'a crucial preventive control' focused on planning, defined RTOs/RPOs, and preparedness activities such as training and simulations.

BCD-18.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of recovery objectives (RTO/RPO), DR test execution and results, and plan currency/training — unlike sibling controls that may focus on detection, access, or configuration; the unique need is quantifying measured recovery performance versus documented targets.

BCD-19.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and currency of the EMOP (percent of critical systems with an up-to-date, tested recovery procedure), frequency and success rate of EMOP tests/drills (count and percent of successful exercises), and operational recovery performance (time-to-restore per system and backup recovery success rate) plus staff training/adherence metrics.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive and requires demonstrating coverage/compliance (e.g., percent of critical systems with tested recovery plans and percent of staff trained). Secondary counts and rates capture drill frequency and backup success; trends show improvement in restore times over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of critical assets that have a current EMOP entry and a passed recovery drill within the past defined interval; count/datetime of drills executed and their outcome (pass/fail); mean and median time-to-restore (RTO) observed during tests; backup success/failure timestamps and last successful recovery test per system; staff training completion and role assignment records.
data_signals: EMOP document version and last review date
risk assessment report identifying threats/vulnerabilities
inventory of critical systems and mapping to recovery procedures
backup completion logs and last successful backup timestamp per system
recovery execution timestamps and measured time-to-restore (RTO) per drill
drill/exercise reports with pass/fail outcome and remediation actions
staff training attendance/completion records and role assignment lists
emergency communication test logs and message delivery/acknowledgement timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated backup and restore logs with timestamps and successful recovery verification, time-stamped drill reports showing measured RTO/RPO, and documented EMOP reviews with sign-offs.
Moderate: regularly scheduled tabletop exercises with documented findings but no full restore verification.
Weak: one-off or undocumented drills, manual notes without timestamps, or only a dated plan file with no test evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description frames the EMOP as a preventive control to ensure continuity and protect data (e.g., 'ensure that healthcare organizations can maintain essential functions' and 'contingency plans in place').

BCD-19.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measuring plan currency and demonstrable recovery capability (tested restores and backup verification) rather than incident detection or post-incident remediation; its metrics emphasize plan coverage, drill outcomes, and measured RTO/RPO performance.

CPL-10.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and frequency of security evaluations for ePHI, count of vulnerabilities and audit findings discovered, and the remediation completion rate and timeliness against the organization's policy for evaluation frequency (e.g., at least annual or when significant changes occur).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes counting vulnerabilities identified and remediated and tracking occurrences of security evaluations (counts). Percentage is useful to express remediation completion rate and coverage; trending is useful to show improvement in effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of security evaluations performed over the audit period and their scope (assets and ePHI systems), number of vulnerabilities/audit findings identified by evaluation (by severity), number and percent of findings remediated with remediation timestamps and resolution evidence, existence and currency of ePHI inventory and risk analysis documents, and documented mitigation plans with status.
data_signals: inventory of ePHI (document or dataset) and last-updated timestamp
risk analysis reports / risk assessment artifacts and timestamps
security evaluation / assessment execution records (dates, scope, assessor)
vulnerability or audit finding records (ID, severity, description, source)
remediation tickets or change requests with opened and closed timestamps
compliance audit results and documented findings
mitigation plans and status (planned, in-progress, completed) with owner
evaluation frequency records (policy stating 'at least annually' or change-triggered evaluations)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated/recorded evaluation runs, time-stamped risk analysis and ePHI inventory, remediation tickets with closure timestamps and evidence, and periodic audit reports showing decreasing open findings over time.
Weak: One-off or ad-hoc assessment reports without timestamps, undocumented inventory, manual summary spreadsheets without traceable remediation records, or missing mitigation plans.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires regular assessment and evaluation to identify vulnerabilities and weaknesses (detective activity) as shown by phrases like 'regularly assessed', 'identify vulnerabilities', and 'risk analysis'.

CPL-10.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on periodic, documented evaluation of controls protecting ePHI and on tracking remediation lifecycle (counts and timeliness), rather than on operational enforcement (e.g., access control) or incident response metrics.

CRY-12.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of PHI-containing assets and communications that are encrypted using approved algorithms (e.g., AES-256), and measure key management compliance (key rotation, access, and revocation) and frequency of encryption audits. Additionally track counts of assets using non-approved algorithms or missing encryption to demonstrate gaps versus the requirement for consistent application across all relevant systems.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and compliance: percentage metrics best show encryption coverage (e.g., % of PHI encrypted). Counts and rates (key rotation rate, unencrypted asset count) and trends are useful to show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of assets and data flows classified as PHI with encryption_at_rest=true and encryption_in_transit=true using approved algorithms; count keys and key events (creation, rotation, revocation) against key policy timelines; list assets with unapproved or missing encryption and the timestamp of last encryption audit. Include patch/upgrade records for encryption libraries and KMS access logs to prove operational secure handling.
data_signals: asset inventory records with PHI classification and storage/transmission location
encryption status flag per asset (at_rest/in_transit) and algorithm metadata (e.g., AES-256)
key management system (KMS) logs: key creation, rotation, access and revocation timestamps and requesting principal
TLS/SSL certificate inventory and enforcement logs for network communications
encryption audit reports and timestamps (audit executions and results)
incident logs showing unauthorized access attempts or breaches involving encrypted PHI
patch/update history for encryption libraries, appliances, and KMS agents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset inventory tied to encryption status and algorithm metadata, continuous KMS logs showing rotations and access, and regularly scheduled encryption audit reports
Moderate: periodic scan results and documented remediation tickets for unencrypted assets
Weak: one-time manual attestations, screenshots, or undocumented statements without KMS logs or inventory linkage

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires implementing encryption and key management to prevent unauthorized reading of PHI (preventive), as shown by phrases like 'rendering it unreadable to unauthorized users' and 'use of strong encryption algorithms'.

CRY-12.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on cryptographic coverage and key lifecycle (encryption algorithms, KMS logs, audit frequency) rather than general access control or incident response, so measurements must include algorithm metadata and key-management events rather than only access logs.

CRY-13.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and correctness of encryption for PHI in transit by reporting the percentage of PHI-bearing transmissions that used approved TLS or other mandated encryption, the count of unencrypted PHI transmissions, and the number of key/certificate issues (expired/weak) that could undermine in-transit protection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage control (encryption must be applied to transmissions), so percentage (coverage %) best shows compliance; counts of unencrypted transmissions and trends of key/certificate issues supplement with event volumes and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percent of network/API/email/file-transfer sessions carrying PHI that negotiated TLS >= configured policy (by protocol and cipher) over total PHI-bearing sessions, (2) count of PHI transmissions detected in cleartext, and (3) inventory counts of expired/weak certificates and key-rotation overdue events. Include timestamps, endpoint identifiers, and policy exception records so queries can group by channel (email/API/file) and asset criticality.
data_signals: TLS/SSL handshake logs with protocol version and cipher suite
network/session metadata tagged as carrying PHI (or DLP classification) with source/destination and timestamp
email gateway logs showing STARTTLS/S/MIME/transport encryption status and attachment scanning results
API gateway/load balancer TLS enforcement logs and endpoint TLS configuration
file-transfer logs (SFTP/FTPS/HTTPS) including plaintext-transfer indicators
certificate inventory with expiration dates, key lengths and signature algorithms
encryption key management logs showing key rotation timestamps and access events
DLP incidents and policy exception records for unencrypted PHI transmissions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated TLS/session logs showing protocol+cipher negotiated and DLP correlation to PHI payloads (demonstrates real-time coverage).
Strong: certificate inventory + key-rotation logs showing no expired/weak keys and regular rotations.
Weak: one-time manual attestations or annual pen-test reports without continuous session evidence.
Weak: screenshots or isolated configuration files without network-level verification that PHI flows are encrypted.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description calls it a "preventive control" to protect PHI during transmission and mandates encryption technologies and policies, indicating prevention of unauthorized disclosure.

CRY-13.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets encryption of PHI in transit (TLS, email/file/API channels, key management) rather than at-rest protection or general cryptographic policy — measurements must therefore focus on session-level TLS/cipher details, DLP-correlated PHI flows, and certificate/key health rather than only storage encryption metrics.

CRY-14.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of ePHI transmissions that use approved integrity controls (validated hashes or digital signatures and approved TLS versions/cipher suites), the count of integrity verification failures detected over time, and the frequency of data-transmission audits/penetration tests verifying integrity controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage/usage verification (e.g., percent of sessions using approved TLS and signed payloads). Counts of violations and trends over time complement coverage metrics to show residual risk and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of ePHI transmission sessions where integrity was verified (session has hash/digital-signature verification and uses an approved TLS version/cipher suite), count integrity verification failures and failed/weak cipher negotiations per period, and record dates/results of transmission-focused audits and penetration tests. Also capture patch/crypto-library version coverage for endpoints handling transmissions.
data_signals: TLS negotiation logs with TLS version and cipher suite per session
hash/digital signature verification events with result (pass/fail), timestamp, session ID, and payload ID
count of integrity verification failures and failed signature verifications per day/week
sessions where TLS was not used or used deprecated/weak ciphers (per-session flags)
certificate validation and revocation check results (OCSP/CRL) and expiration events
patch/inventory records for crypto libraries (e.g., OpenSSL/Schannel) and last-patch timestamp per host
data transmission policy version and last review timestamp
penetration test / audit reports focused on data-in-transit with findings and remediation status
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, continuous integrity-verification logs (per-session hash/signature validation with session IDs) are strong evidence
Correlation between failing verification events and remediation tickets/patch updates strengthens evidence
Periodic penetration-test reports and audit logs add corroboration but are weaker if isolated (annual) and not tied to continuous telemetry
Screenshots or one-off manual tests are weak evidence compared to machine-generated session-level logs and configuration inventories

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a "preventive control" and mandates cryptographic measures (hashing, digital signatures, TLS) to prevent tampering in transit.

CRY-14.differentiation_note: Unlike controls focused on at-rest encryption or key management, this control specifically requires session-level integrity verification (per-transmission hash/signature and TLS negotiation metrics) and continuous transmission logs rather than only configuration or inventory evidence.

CRY-15.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of electronic communications containing PHI that use approved encryption in transit (by channel: email, file transfer, remote access) and track counts of transmission-related security incidents and misconfigurations (e.g., weak TLS versions, unencrypted transfers). Provide evidence of endpoint and transport configuration compliance and key transport/protocol usage over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage measurement (what percent of PHI transmissions are encrypted) so percentage is primary; counts of incidents/unencrypted transfers and rates/trends over time are useful secondary metrics to show enforcement and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of PHI-bearing transmissions that used approved encryption protocols per channel and time window (e.g., daily/weekly), and count transmission-related security incidents and misconfigurations (e.g., TLS downgraded/disabled occurrences). Include endpoint/agent configuration records showing enforcement, protocol/cipher telemetry from TLS handshakes, and VPN/SFTP usage logs to allow queries by asset and source/destination.
data_signals: TLS/SSL handshake logs with protocol version and cipher suite
SMTP/MTA logs indicating STARTTLS or opportunistic TLS usage and encryption success/failure
SFTP/FTPS transfer logs with transfer timestamps, source/destination, and encryption status
VPN connection logs showing encryption ciphers and successful secure tunnels
DLP or gateway logs that tag messages as containing PHI and whether transmission was encrypted
endpoint configuration records for email clients, transfer tools and enforcement of secure transmission settings
inventory of software versions for transmission tools and patch/upgrade timestamps
incident records for breaches or interception events related to transmission (security incident tickets)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated telemetry showing TLS/SFTP/VPN encryption success for PHI-tagged transmissions and endpoint configuration enforcement logs; paired with routine scans/pen tests confirming no downgrade/weak-cipher acceptance.
Weak: periodic manual attestations or single point-in-time screenshots of settings, or annual penetration test reports alone that don't show ongoing enforcement or per-transfer evidence.
Moderate: periodic audit reports combined with some transport logs but missing PHI-tag correlation or lacking protocol/cipher detail.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description calls this a 'preventive control designed to ensure that all electronic communications containing PHI are encrypted', indicating measures to prevent interception rather than detect after the fact.

CRY-15.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on encryption and secure transmission of PHI across channels (email, SFTP, VPN) and requires per-transfer/protocol telemetry and PHI-tag correlation — unlike sibling controls that might target key management or at-rest encryption, this one requires transport-level evidence and channel-specific metrics.

DCH-21.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of PHI records with an assigned retention period and automated enforcement, the count of PHI items past their retention end that remain not securely disposed, and the mean time from retention expiry to documented secure disposal (by disposal method).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that verifies coverage and policy adherence (best expressed as percentages of records/systems in compliance). Counts of over-retained records and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show remediation velocity and recurrence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (A) % of PHI records/assets that have a retention_period defined in the retention policy table and an associated retention_end_timestamp; (B) count of PHI records where retention_end_timestamp < today and no disposal_event record exists; (C) median/mean time (days) between retention_end_timestamp and disposal_event.timestamp grouped by disposal_method and asset type; (D) number of retention policy exceptions and non-compliance incidents in the review period.
data_signals: retention_policy table: retention_period and retention_category by PHI type
asset and data inventory mapping indicating PHI locations and identifiers
record-level retention_end_timestamp and retention_start_timestamp
disposal_event logs with timestamp, disposal_method, and certificate_of_destruction flag
automated lifecycle job execution logs (job_id, run_timestamp, success/failure, affected_record_count)
backup and archive retention metadata showing retention durations and deletion timestamps
audit records of periodic retention reviews and findings
non-compliance incident logs referencing over-retained PHI and remediation actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated lifecycle deletion logs and disposal_event records with timestamps and disposal_method (and certificates) plus corresponding retention_end_timestamp entries and system-enforced retention rules.
Strong: end-to-end mapping that shows backups/archives honored retention policies (deletion timestamps in backup metadata).
Weak: annual/manual spreadsheets or a one-time attestation without machine-generated disposal logs or asset-level retention_end timestamps.
Weak: anecdotal training completion without linkage to retention actions or documented disposal events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes establishing policies, retention periods and secure disposal methods (preventing over-retention) and recommends automated lifecycle enforcement and audits.

DCH-21.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about defining retention schedules and demonstrating secure disposal of PHI (including backups and state-specific rules) — unlike sibling controls that may focus on access or encryption, its measurements require record-level retention_end timestamps, disposal logs, and lifecycle-job execution evidence.

END-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of workstations that meet the defined security baseline (MFA enabled, EDR/antivirus installed and up-to-date, disk encryption enabled, firewall configured) and track counts/rates of workstation-specific unauthorized access attempts and security incidents plus employee training completion rates for secure workstation practices.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/compliance (e.g., percent of workstations with EDR, MFA, encryption). Percentages show coverage; counts and trends supplement with incident volumes and change over time (patch/agent deployment).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of all inventoryed workstations that meet the workstation security baseline: EDR agent installed and reporting in the last 24h, antivirus signatures updated in the last 24h, personal firewall enabled and configured, full-disk encryption active, and MFA enabled for workstation access; measure count/rate of workstation authentication failures/unauthorized access attempts and number of workstation-related security incidents; measure employee completion rate of workstation security training.
data_signals: endpoint inventory with device ID and ePHI-access flag
MFA enrollment / last-successful-authentication logs for workstation accounts
EDR agent installation status and last-checkin timestamp per device
antivirus definition/update timestamps and last full-scan result per device
full-disk encryption status (BitLocker/FileVault) and encryption key presence per device
workstation firewall configuration status and last configuration change
workstation authentication failure events and counts (unauthorized access attempts) with timestamps
workstation security training completion records with user IDs and completion timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized endpoint management/EDR telemetry showing agent health and configuration for all devices (continuous agent check-ins, configuration inventory, automated compliance reports).
Strong: authentication logs showing MFA-enforced access and aggregated counts of failed attempts tied to device IDs.
Weak: periodic manual attestations or one-off screenshots of configurations, and annual test reports without continuous agent telemetry.
Weak: incomplete asset inventory or missing device-to-ePHI access mapping that prevents verifying coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' and focuses on measures to 'ensure that all workstations ... are secured' (MFA, EDR, encryption, physical restrictions).

END-5.differentiation_note: This control is device- and user-behavior-focused (workstation configuration, EDR, MFA, training) and requires endpoint inventory and agent telemetry for measurement, unlike sibling controls that may focus on network, application, or project-level security.

GOV-34.measurement_goal: Measure that a qualified HIPAA Security Officer is appointed with documented authority and senior leadership access, and that the officer executes core responsibilities: regular risk assessments, policy lifecycle management, workforce security training completion, and documented incident oversight (counts and remediations) against organizational policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive/governance control where coverage and completion rates (percentages) — e.g., percent of org units with role assignment, percent of policies reviewed on schedule, training completion % — best demonstrate compliance; counts and trends supplement with incident volumes and assessment frequency, while a score can summarize maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics: (1) presence and currency of an appointed Security Officer record (binary) and percent of organizational units with an assigned security contact; (2) number and timestamps of completed risk assessments per required period; (3) percent of security policies with documented review/approval dates within policy cycle; (4) workforce security training completion rate and dates; (5) count of security incidents reported and incidents where Security Officer was recorded as responder or approver; (6) documentation artifacts (role description, delegation of authority, org chart entry, meeting minutes) with timestamps.
data_signals: appointed Security Officer record (name, start date, role description, signed delegation)
organizational chart entry showing direct access to senior leadership
risk assessment reports with execution timestamps and author
security policy documents with review/approval timestamps and version history
training enrollment and completion records (user, course, completion date, score)
security incident reports/logs indicating reporting date, reporter, Security Officer involvement and resolution timestamps
project plan templates or sign-off logs showing Security Officer review/approval
meeting minutes or communications (emails/notices) that publish policies or decisions by the Security Officer
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed role description and delegation documents stored in a versioned repository, automated training platform completion records, timestamped risk assessment reports, and incident logs showing Security Officer involvement.
Weak: verbal attestations, undated or single-instance documents, annual summary emails without timestamps, or manual spreadsheets lacking user and timestamp metadata.
Strong: consistent trending improvement or sustained compliance versus policy thresholds (e.g., training completion >= policy target each period) rather than one-off snapshots.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires appointment and oversight to prevent noncompliance and mismanagement by establishing policy, authority, and proactive activities (risk assessments, training, policy enforcement).

GOV-34.differentiation_note: This control is organizational/governance-focused (role and accountability) rather than a technical detection or remediation control; measurements must emphasize personnel appointment records, policy lifecycle metrics, training completion, and Security Officer involvement in projects and incidents rather than system telemetry.

GOV-35.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of HIPAA-aligned policies for all PHI-handling areas (percent of required policies published and approved), the timeliness of policy reviews (percent reviewed on schedule), and workforce acknowledgement and training coverage (employee acknowledgement and training completion rates) to demonstrate documented, implemented procedures for protecting PHI.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control focused on coverage and compliance: auditors need coverage percentages (policy coverage, acknowledgement rate) as the primary measure; counts (number of overdue reviews, trainings) and trends (review completion over time) are useful secondary signals and a compliance score can summarize posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Quantify (1) percent of required HIPAA/PHI-related policies that are published, approved, and assigned an owner; (2) percent of policies reviewed within their scheduled review period; (3) employee acknowledgement completion rate per policy; and (4) training sessions delivered and assessment pass rates tied to specific policies. Include timestamps, approver IDs, and mapping of each policy to PHI handling areas to allow asset-level queries.
data_signals: policy registry entries with policy_id, title, owner, status (draft/published/retired), approval_timestamp, approver_id
policy version history and policy_revision timestamps with author/approver
policy review schedule and last_review_date vs. review_due_date
employee policy acknowledgement records (user_id, policy_id, acknowledgement_timestamp)
training session records and attendance lists (session_id, user_id, completion_timestamp)
training assessment scores and pass/fail per user linked to policy topics
mapping of policies to PHI-handling processes/assets (asset_id -> policy_id)
exceptions/waiver records and documented approvals for policy deviations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy registry with approver IDs, signed/dated approvals, automated employee acknowledgement logs, training attendance and assessment records, and explicit mapping of policies to PHI assets and review due dates.
Weak: isolated MS Word/PDF copies without approval metadata, ad-hoc email distribution, manual spreadsheets without timestamps, or one-off training without assessment results.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on establishing, documenting, implementing and reviewing policies and procedures (preventive governance) to avoid PHI breaches and staff misunderstanding.

GOV-35.differentiation_note: This control is governance-focused and measures documentation, approvals, review cadence, and acknowledgements for PHI-related policies — unlike technical controls it requires records (policy registry, approvals, acknowledgments, training evidence) rather than system enforcement telemetry.

GOV-36.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of required information security policies and procedures that are published, approved, and accessible to personnel (with current review/approval metadata), plus the percentage of personnel who have received/acknowledged key policies and the count of policies overdue for review or approval.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about coverage and availability: auditors need coverage percentages (what percent of required policies are published/approved/acknowledged). Counts and trends are useful secondary metrics to track number of overdue policies and review cadence over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of all required information security policies and procedures that are (a) published in the canonical repository, (b) have a recorded approver and last review date within the policy-defined review period, and (c) are marked as available to staff; plus count/percentage of staff who have completed policy acknowledgements or related training. Flag policies with missing approval, missing review metadata, or overdue review dates.
data_signals: policy document repository records with metadata (policy_id, title, publish_date, last_review_date, next_review_due, approver_id, version)
accessibility/visibility listing from intranet or policy portal (document URL, access permissions)
employee policy acknowledgement logs (employee_id, policy_id, acknowledgement_timestamp)
policy publication/distribution records (email/intranet post timestamps and recipient lists)
policy version history / change log entries (who changed, change_timestamp, change_reason)
policy exceptions register or approvals pending (policy_id, exception_reason, approver)
training completion records tied to policy topics (employee_id, course_id, completion_timestamp)
count of policies mapped to remote work/insecure-remote-work guidance (policy_id, scope_tag)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: canonical policy repository entries with structured metadata (approver, publish_date, last_review_date), automated employee acknowledgements tied to SSO, and issuance/distribution logs showing recipients.
Strong: continuous linkage of policies to training completion records and an exceptions register documenting approved deviations.
Weak: ad-hoc PDF copies on shared drives without approval metadata, one-off emailed policies without acknowledgement tracking, or annual manual attestations only.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control ensures policies and procedures are available, reviewed and communicated (prevents personnel from lacking guidance) — aligned with preventive controls that verify coverage and distribution.

GOV-36.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls (e.g., access or endpoint controls), this control focuses on document lifecycle, approval metadata, and personnel communication/acknowledgement; measurement must emphasize repository metadata, review cadence, and acknowledgement records rather than system logs.

GOV-37.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of required information security policies and procedures that are created, approved, published to the official repository, assigned an owner, reviewed within the policy cycle, and acknowledged by responsible personnel; plus verify presence and currency of incident/breach contact lists and that security requirements are referenced in project plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive policy-management control — auditors need coverage and currency percentages (e.g., % of policies reviewed/approved/published on schedule). Counts (number of missing/unpublished policies, contact list entries) and trending (review cadence over time) add operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A policy inventory record per required policy (policy_id) with owner, publication location, creation/review/approval timestamps, defined review frequency, and employee acknowledgment records; additionally, an up-to-date breach/incident contact list with last-updated timestamp and mapping references from project plans to applicable security policies.
data_signals: policy inventory (policy_id, title, scope)
policy owner field
policy creation_date, last_review_date, approval_date
defined review_frequency or next_review_date
publication URL or repository path for each policy
employee acknowledgment records (user_id, policy_id, acknowledgment_timestamp)
incident/breach contact list entries (name, role, contact, last_updated_timestamp)
project plan artifacts or JIRA/Confluence links referencing policy_id or security requirements
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy management system records showing approval timestamps, owner assignment, publication path, and automated employee acknowledgments; mapping links from project artifacts to policy IDs; contact list with recent last_updated timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc or manual Word/PDF files without metadata, single point-in-time screenshots of a policy, or anecdotal statements from staff without timestamped acknowledgments.

affinity_keywords: personnel don't understand information security requirements

control_type_classification.reasoning: The associated risk text emphasizes failure to 'define, review, approve, publish, share, or communicate Information Security Policies' which indicates this control prevents governance gaps by ensuring policies exist and are maintained.

GOV-37.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on policy lifecycle and communication (existence, review/approval, publication, acknowledgments and mapping to projects/contacts), unlike operational controls that measure technical controls or incident detection — measurement must include policy metadata and acknowledgment records; note: control description was not provided, so associated risk texts were used as the primary source for measurement cues.

GOV-38.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage, currency, approval and communication of required information security policies and procedures: percent of required policies that are documented and approved, percent published/shared to personnel, percent of policies reviewed on schedule, and count/rate of employee acknowledgements against defined policy distribution requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about coverage and compliance (documented/approved/published), so percentage metrics (coverage, review-compliance, acknowledgement rates) best show compliance; counts and trend metrics support operational visibility over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of required policy and procedure documents vs. documented/published artifacts, approval metadata (approver id and timestamp), last review timestamps, distribution/publishing events, and employee acknowledgement records. Break down by policy owner, policy type (e.g., remote work, project security), and whether each policy is referenced in project plans or role-responsibility matrices.
data_signals: policy repository records with metadata (policy title, owner, version, approval timestamp)
policy review schedule and last review timestamp per policy
publication/distribution logs (intranet postings, email distribution receipts)
employee acknowledgement/attestation logs with user ID and timestamp
list of required policies mapped to organizational policy register
project plan templates showing presence/absence of policy references
role/responsibility matrix mapping policies to owners
count of policies overdue for review and remediation action items
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized policy repository with structured metadata (approval timestamps, owners), automated distribution records and time-stamped employee attestations; regular review cycle logs showing scheduled vs actual review dates.
Weak: scattered PDFs without approval metadata, ad-hoc email copies without acknowledgement tracking, or manual lists that don't show timestamps for approval/review.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires documenting, approving, publishing and communicating policies — actions that prevent misunderstandings and mitigate future incidents, which is characteristic of preventive controls.

GOV-38.differentiation_note: This control focuses on existence, approval and communication of policy artifacts and personnel acknowledgement (documentation lifecycle), unlike operational controls that measure technical enforcement or incident response — measurement must capture metadata and distribution/acknowledgement signals rather than system logs.

GOV-39.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of applicable information security policies and procedures that have been reviewed and updated within their required review cycle, track the count of policies overdue for review, and measure dissemination/acknowledgement of the latest published versions and currency of breach/response contact lists.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about coverage and currency (who/what has been updated), so percentage metrics (e.g., % policies current) best show compliance; counts and trends supplement with overdue counts and improvement over review cycles.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of information security policies with revision_date within the defined review interval (grouped by policy owner and policy type), count of policies with last_revision_date older than their review_period, timestamps and approver IDs for the latest approval, publication/distribution timestamps, employee acknowledgement counts for the current version, and last_updated timestamp for breach/response contact lists.
data_signals: policy revision timestamp / last_revision_date
policy next_review_date or review_schedule
policy owner identifier
approval timestamp and approver identity (approval signature record)
policy publication/distribution timestamp (e.g., intranet publish log)
employee acknowledgement receipts for current policy version
breach contact list last_updated timestamp and contact entries
project plan records or change requests showing security sign-off or inclusion of policy references
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled policy repository with immutable timestamps, recorded approver signatures, automated publication logs, and employee acknowledgement receipts indicating distribution.
Weak: single ad-hoc email announcing changes, undocumented manual edits, missing approver or timestamp metadata, or contact lists without last_updated timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates keeping policies and procedures current and communicated (review/approve/publish/communicate), which is proactive and intended to prevent governance failures.

GOV-39.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the currency, approval, publication, and distribution/acknowledgement of policies and contact lists (governance artifacts), unlike sibling operational controls that measure implementation of technical safeguards; it uniquely requires measuring policy revision timestamps, approval records, and acknowledgement receipts.

GOV-40.measurement_goal: Measure the volume of reported security violations, the proportion investigated and remediated within policy-defined timeframes, time-to-resolution for violations, and evidence of documented outcomes (investigation notes, remediation actions, disciplinary steps).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control focuses on managing and closing violations (a corrective activity) so rates (e.g., closure rate, percent closed within SLA) show effectiveness; counts and percentages supplement with volume and coverage and trends show improvements over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of security violation reports per period; for each report, capture timestamps for report creation, investigation start, remediation completion; classification of violation type and responsible owner; SLA or policy threshold for resolution; documentation of outcomes (remediation actions, disciplinary records, policy updates). Aggregate metrics: percent investigated, percent remediated within SLA, mean time to remediate, repeat-violation counts by user or system.
data_signals: violation incident/ticket ID and creation timestamp
investigation start and close timestamps (ticket state transitions)
remediation action records and remediation completion timestamp
violation classification/type and severity label
owner/assignee for investigation and remediation
SLA/policy resolution target by violation type
disciplinary action or HR record references tied to violation ID
evidence of policy communication/acknowledgement (publication, share, acknowledgement timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous ticketing system logs with structured timestamps, remediation actions and attachments, SLA fields, and linked disciplinary records
Strong: searchable investigation notes and closure evidence tied to ticket IDs
Weak: annual manual summaries, undocumented or ad-hoc spreadsheets, or point-in-time screenshots without timestamps
Weak: absence of policy/SLA reference or missing assignment/closure timestamps

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Security violations managed' implies post-incident handling, investigation and remediation activities, which are corrective controls.

GOV-40.differentiation_note: This control is about the end-to-end handling and closure of violations (investigation, remediation, disciplinary outcomes) rather than creating policies or defining responsibilities; measurements must capture ticket-level lifecycle and SLA compliance rather than policy existence alone.

GOV-41.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of assigned information-security responsibilities: percentage of policies, project plans, remote/mobile-device programs, and operational processes that have a named security owner and documented approval/review metadata; and count of unassigned or overdue ownership assignments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control where auditors need coverage % (e.g., percent of policies/projects with an assigned owner). Counts of unassigned items and trend over time (assignment rate) are useful secondary measures to show remediation and ongoing compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of active information security policies, active projects, mobile/remote work programs, and critical system components that have a named security owner in the authoritative repository, plus the count of items lacking an owner and timestamps of owner assignment and last review. Include approval/communication records showing policy publication and acknowledgement where applicable.
data_signals: policy repository records with 'owner' field and approval/review timestamp
policy publication/distribution logs and employee acknowledgement receipts
project plan metadata indicating 'security owner' or 'security task assignee'
RACI matrix or role assignment table entries for security responsibilities
mobile device program or remote-work policy owner entries and device custodian assignments
org chart or HR job description entries mapping roles to security responsibilities
policy review cycle logs showing review/approval dates and assigned reviewers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative policy repository entries with explicit owner field, signed/recorded approvals, project plans with filled 'security owner' metadata, and periodic review records.
Weak: informal emails, verbal assignment notes, or scattered spreadsheet lists without timestamps or authoritative linkage to policies/projects.
Strong: continuous tracking that shows assignment and subsequent review timestamps vs. weak one-off confirmations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Assigning and documenting security responsibilities is a preventive governance activity (addresses 'responsibilities have not been defined or allocated' and ensures owners exist before incidents occur).

GOV-41.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls, this control focuses on documented assignment and governance artifacts (policy owners, project plan owner fields, RACI entries) rather than technical telemetry; measurement must combine policy repository, project metadata, and HR/organizational records.

GOV-42.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of corporate workstations covered by an approved, published workstation security policy and the percentage of employees who have acknowledged that policy, plus counts of workstations that are non‑compliant with the approved configuration baseline and documented exceptions against the policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (policies implemented) so coverage and acknowledgement percentages best show policy reach; counts and compliance scores provide complementary evidence and trend shows review/update cadence over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of managed workstation assets with configuration profiles that match the approved workstation security baseline (by asset ID and scan timestamp). 2) Percentage of active employees who have a recorded policy acknowledgement timestamp for the published workstation security policy and the latest policy version. 3) Count of published workstation policy documents with approval metadata and last-review date, and count of active exceptions/waivers with approval and expiry.
data_signals: published workstation policy documents with version, approval signature/approver ID, and publication date
policy distribution logs or communication receipts (email or intranet) and employee acknowledgement timestamps
endpoint configuration scan results showing baseline compliance by asset ID and timestamp
inventory of managed vs unmanaged workstations (asset registry) with ownership and last-seen timestamp
