exceptions/waiver records with approver, rationale, and expiry date
policy review cycle records and last review/next review due date
remote access configuration logs for remote workstations (VPN client versions, endpoint posture checks)
counts of non-compliant workstations and remediation ticket timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated endpoint configuration scan logs correlated to asset inventory and per-asset compliance status over time; employee acknowledgement records with timestamps tied to published policy versions; documented approval metadata and periodic review logs.

Weak: single point-in-time screenshots of the policy, or manually assembled lists without timestamps; narrative statements that policies exist but no publication, distribution, or compliance evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates implementation of workstation security policies (prevention through policy definition, publication, and enforcement), which is inherently preventive.

GOV-42.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls that might focus solely on technical endpoint configuration or incident detection, this control uniquely requires evidence of policy lifecycle (approval, publication, employee acknowledgement) plus enforcement by measuring workstation baseline compliance and documented exceptions.

HRS-17.measurement_goal: Measure the rate and coverage of sanction actions applied when policy violations occur: count of confirmed policy violations, count and rate of incidents where the sanction policy was applied, timeliness between confirmed violation and sanction application, and percentage of affected personnel with documented sanction outcomes linked to the governing policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about applying sanctions (an action after an event), so measuring the rate of violations that result in sanctioned outcomes is primary; percentages (coverage and policy acknowledgment) and counts (total sanctions, violations) provide supporting context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of confirmed policy violations by type and the subset that resulted in a documented sanction, timestamps for violation detection and sanction application to calculate time-to-sanction, and percentage of violations with completed sanction documentation tied to employee/contractor records and policy references. Also measure coverage: percent of staff with signed acknowledgment of the sanction policy and percent of termination records that include communicated confidentiality commitments.
data_signals: incident/violation records with violation type and confirmation timestamp
HR disciplinary action records with sanction type, decision timestamp, and case ID
mapping between incident IDs and sanction case IDs (linkage evidence)
policy acknowledgement receipts/signed agreements per employee
termination checklist entries indicating confidentiality commitments were communicated
background-check completion records (where applicable to sanction decisions)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage of incident record to HR sanction case with timestamps and policy clause referenced; consistent sanction outcomes across similar violation types; signed policy acknowledgements and termination checklist entries stored in HR system.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads or undocumented verbal disciplinary actions; single anecdotal examples without system-recorded timestamps or linkage to the governing policy.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Sanction policy applied' implies actions taken in response to violations (application of sanctions), which is corrective in nature.

HRS-17.differentiation_note: Unlike policy-definition controls, HRS-17 focuses on the enforcement/action side—measurement must track incident-to-sanction linkage, timeliness, and documentation in HR/incident systems rather than just existence or publication of policies.

IAC-24.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of access grants and active accounts that were provisioned or modified only after documented, approved authorization (approval records, approver identity, timestamp), and the count/rate of unauthorized or ad-hoc access grants detected over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive authorization control, auditors need coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., % of grants with approved requests). Counts and rates of unauthorized grants and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show incidents and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of access grants in a period that have a corresponding documented approval record (requestor, approver, approval timestamp, justification) and (2) count and rate of access grants performed outside the documented provisioning workflow (ad-hoc grants). Also track number of active privileged accounts lacking owner or expiration and the completion rate of periodic access reviews.
data_signals: access request and approval records (requestor, approver, timestamp, justification)
identity provider / IAM provisioning event logs (create/modify/delete) with actor and timestamp
user account inventory with owner, role/entitlement, status (active/disabled) and expiration
privileged account / privileged role assignment list with justification and expiry
access review / attestation completion records and attestor identities
authentication logs and remote access (VPN/session) logs showing successful authentications and source
exceptions or manual change tickets for out-of-band access grants
incident records for confirmed unauthorized access or breach events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated provisioning logs joined to formal approval records (request + approver + timestamp) and programmatic enforcement (permission only granted after approval).
Strong: periodic attestation records showing reviewers, scope, and remediation of findings.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or ad-hoc email approvals without timestamps or linkage to provisioning events.
Weak: one-off point-in-time screenshots or attestations without historical trend data or linkage to IAM logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Access authorized' and associated risks about 'unauthorized' access and 'access control' indicate it's intended to prevent unauthorized access by requiring authorization before granting access.

IAC-24.differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on evidence of prior authorization and proper provisioning (approval records, provisioning logs, attestation) rather than detection of incidents or remediation timelines, which siblings may emphasize.

IAC-25.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of access controls across systems: percentage of active accounts with approved role/permission assignments, count and rate of unauthorized access attempts detected, and timely removal of access for terminated or changed users against policy-defined timeframes.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control focuses on applied coverage and enforcement (preventive) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) best show scope; counts and rates complement with volumes of unauthorized attempts and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percent of active user accounts whose current role/permission assignments match an approved access entitlement record; (2) count and rate of failed/unauthorized authentication or authorization events per period; (3) time-to-revoke access for terminated or role-changed users (provisioning/deprovisioning latency); and (4) completion rate of scheduled access reviews/attestations.
data_signals: user account provisioning/deprovisioning timestamps with actor and request id
role/permission assignment records and approver attestation entries
authentication/authorization logs (success/failure) with timestamp, user id, resource, source IP
privileged account inventory and last-used timestamps
access review/attestation records with completion dates and scope
remote access/VPN connection logs and connection source metadata (to address insecure remote work sites)
access exception or access-change ticket records with approvals
terminated user access removal audit logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated logs showing authz/authn events and automated provisioning/deprovisioning are strong evidence
Recent access review attestations with machine-verifiable records (approver id, timestamp, scope) are strong
Ad-hoc spreadsheets, infrequent manual attestations, or one-time screenshots are weak evidence
Absence of deprovisioning timestamps or lack of approver metadata weakens evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Access controls applied' implies controls enacted to prevent unauthorized access, so this is primarily preventive (enforcement and coverage).

IAC-25.differentiation_note: This control is about operational enforcement and coverage of access controls across systems (provisioning, logs, reviews) rather than incident handling or vulnerability scanning; it uniquely requires measurement of entitlement coverage and provisioning/deprovisioning latency. (If more specific access-control sub-controls exist, this entry covers broad enforcement and coverage.)

IAC-26.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user accounts and roles whose access was provisioned with documented approval, the percentage of active accounts covered by a completed access review within the policy review window, the mean time to modify or revoke access after an approved change or termination, and the count of segregation-of-duties (SoD) conflicts identified and remediated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control centers on coverage and timeliness (provisioning, reviews, modifications) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) best show policy adherence; counts and trends show volumes and improvement over time and rates measure timeliness (e.g., revocation within SLA).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) % of active user accounts with a recorded approved access request and entitlement assignment; (b) % of scoped accounts with an access review completed within the policy window (e.g., last 90 days); (c) mean time between termination (or change request) and access revocation; (d) count of outstanding SoD conflicts and count remediated within SLA.
data_signals: access provisioning/request records (requestor, approver, timestamp, requested entitlements)
access modification/change logs with timestamp and approver
access review reports (scope, reviewer, date, outcome) and last_review_date per account
user lifecycle events (hire/termination/deactivation timestamps) mapped to access revocation timestamps
current entitlement/role membership per user including privileged accounts
segregation-of-duties (SoD) conflict reports and exception approvals
orphaned/inactive account listings and privileged account inventory
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped provisioning and revocation logs correlated with HR termination events and signed/dated access review reports stored in a centralized system
Strong: continuous entitlement inventory that shows last_review_date per account and links to approval records
Weak: point-in-time screenshots of spreadsheets or one-off manual attestations without timestamps or approver identities
Weak: annual manual review reports alone that cannot be correlated to provisioning/revocation events

control_type_classification.reasoning: The wording 'Access established' and 'modified' implies preventive controls over granting/modifying access; 'reviewed' introduces detective elements but the control primarily enforces correct provisioning and timely modification (preventive).

IAC-26.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of end-to-end access lifecycle (provisioning approvals, periodic reviews, and modification/revocation timeliness) and SoD conflict counts — unlike siblings that might focus only on privileged access auditing or remote access configuration, it uniquely combines provisioning, review coverage, and modification latency. If scope is broader in practice, map privileged-access controls separately.

IAC-27.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of workforce identities with documented authorization and assigned supervisors/manager attestations, and count unauthorized access incidents or access assignments lacking approval to demonstrate workforce access is authorized and supervised per policy.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive — auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of workforce with documented authorization/supervision). Counts of unauthorized access events and rates of missing approvals are useful secondary measures to show failures and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Current ratio of active workforce accounts that have a documented authorization record (signed/recorded approval) and an assigned supervisor/manager plus count of active accounts without authorization or supervisory assignment; number and rate of unauthorized access incidents and access revocations over the reporting period. Include provenance (who approved, timestamp) and linkage to HR identity records and provisioning events.
data_signals: user account provisioning records with approval metadata (approver id, timestamp)
HRIS manager/supervisor assignment field for each employee
authorization approval documents or signed attestations linked to user id
access revocation/deprovisioning logs with timestamps
unauthorized access incident records / breach incident tickets
remote work security attestations / remote device compliance checks tied to user
periodic access review completion reports (reviewer, date, exceptions list)
provisioning change request logs (who requested, approver, justification)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated provisioning records tied to HRIS showing approver id and timestamp plus access review reports demonstrating 100% coverage; machine-readable authorization metadata.
Moderate: signed PDF approvals or recorded manager attestations with timestamps but not machine-linked to provisioning logs.
Weak: ad-hoc email approvals without consistent linkage to account provisioning, or one-off attestations without periodic reviews.
Weak: only anecdotal assurances or no incident/revocation logs when breaches are alleged.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Workforce authorized and/or supervised' implies controls to prevent unauthorized access by ensuring authorization and supervision before or during access (preventive).

IAC-27.differentiation_note: This control focuses on evidence of documented authorization and supervisory assignment for people (HR/provisioning linkage), unlike sibling controls that may focus on technical access enforcement, logging, or incident response — measurement must tie identities to approval and supervision records.

IAC-28.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of interactive sessions and remote connections that are terminated automatically according to the organization's idle-time or session timeout policy, plus the count and rate of sessions that exceeded the configured timeout (indicating failures or exceptions).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires verifying coverage/enforcement (coverage % of systems with auto log-off configured). Secondary metrics (counts/rates/trends) show volumes of auto-logoffs, exceptions, and changes over time to detect operational gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) number of sessions terminated by automated log-off within the configured timeout window divided by total interactive/remote sessions, (2) count of sessions where last-activity > configured idle timeout but no automated termination occurred, and (3) list of systems/devices with the auto-logoff configuration and their configured timeout values.
data_signals: automated log-off / session termination event logs with timestamp, user ID, device/endpoint ID, session_id
session start and last-activity timestamps per session
configured idle timeout or session timeout setting per system/profile
count of interactive/remote sessions per device/platform (total sessions)
exceptions or override events (administrative whitelist or explicit session persistence flags)
inventory of systems showing whether auto log-off is enabled (boolean) and configured timeout value
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated session termination logs correlated with configuration inventory showing auto-logoff enabled and configured timeout matching policy.
Strong: low count/rate of sessions exceeding timeout and documented exceptions.
Weak: periodic manual attestations or screenshots of settings without event logs, or single point-in-time checks of a subset of systems.
Weak: absence of configuration inventory or inability to correlate termination events to specific system configs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Log-off automated' indicates a preventive enforcement mechanism (automatic session termination) intended to prevent unauthorized access or exposure.

IAC-28.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of automated session termination/configuration and runtime termination events (coverage and enforcement), distinguishing it from sibling access controls that focus on authentication, authorization, or audit logging; note: source text was minimal so keywords and signals were inferred from the control name and associated risk descriptions.

IAC-29.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and enforcement of documented emergency (break‑glass) access procedures by counting emergency access activations, verifying that each activation has an authorized approval record, is time‑limited and revoked, and that a post‑event review was completed; and measure the percentage of emergency access events that meet those procedural requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is procedural/preventive so auditors want coverage/compliance percentages (e.g., percent of emergency accesses with required approvals). Event counts and rates are needed as secondary measures to show volumes and remediation velocity and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of emergency (break‑glass) access activations per system, with linked approval ticket (approver id, timestamp), session start and end timestamps, whether automated revocation occurred, role/privilege scope granted, and completion status of a post‑event review; compute the percentage of activations that have all required artifacts and were revoked within the policy time limit.
data_signals: break‑glass / emergency access activation event logs with userID, target system, timestamp
authorization/approval records or tickets linked to activation (ticket ID, approver, approval timestamp, justification)
privileged session start and end timestamps (duration) and automated revocation logs
inventory of accounts/roles flagged with emergency access capability
emergency access policy document version and effective date
post‑event review records or incident/retrospective reports linked to each activation
linked incident ticket IDs and remediation/closure timestamps
exceptions or waiver records for emergency access events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, immutable break‑glass event logs with linked approval ticket IDs, enforced time‑limited sessions with automated revocation logs, and completed post‑event reviews for each activation.
Weak: ad‑hoc manual spreadsheets or email approvals, one‑off retrospective approvals, missing session end timestamps, or undocumented justifications.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishment of procedures to control emergency access (procedures and preventative controls to ensure access is authorized, time‑limited and logged).

IAC-29.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on temporary/emergency (break‑glass) access — measurement must emphasize event-level logging, approval linkage, time‑limited revocation, and post‑event review rather than routine access provisioning or periodic access review coverage.

IAC-30.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of employee termination/offboarding actions: percentage of terminated users whose accounts, credentials, and physical access were revoked within the policy-defined timeframe, plus counts of exceptions and orphaned accounts attributable to terminated identities.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Termination/offboarding is a coverage-and-timeliness control; auditors want percent coverage (e.g., percent of terminations completed on-time) as primary evidence, with counts of failures and trends over time to show recurring issues and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate (1) the percent of HR termination events where IAM account deactivation, privileged role removal, and physical access (badge) deactivation occurred within the organization policy timeframe; (2) the distribution/count of termination events with delayed or missing deprovisioning; and (3) the number of active accounts linked to persons with termination dates (or no active employment) to identify orphaned accounts. Include timestamps from HR and IAM systems to compute time-to-deprovision per event.
data_signals: HR termination event records with employee ID and termination timestamp
IAM account deactivation logs with actor, target account, and timestamp
privileged role removal/change events (role change logs) with timestamps
physical access/badge deactivation records with timestamps
device return or asset decommission records linked to employee ID
last-login timestamps for accounts correlated to termination date
exceptions or approved-delay records for deprovisioning
orphaned/active account inventory cross-referenced with HR status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated correlation of HR termination timestamp with IAM/badge deactivation timestamps showing deprovisioning within policy SLA for each event.
Strong: system-generated logs and automation runbooks showing successful offboarding steps per employee.
Weak: manual email confirmations or one-off screenshots without machine-readable timestamps linking HR and IAM events.
Weak: annual attestations alone that do not include per-termination timestamps or exception records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Termination procedures are preventive controls intended to remove access and prevent unauthorized access after employment ends (timely deprovisioning and role revocation).

IAC-30.differentiation_note: This control specifically measures offboarding completeness and timeliness (per-termination deprovisioning and orphaned-account detection), unlike sibling access controls that may focus on periodic access reviews or privileged access management.

IAC-31.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and timeliness of access provisioning and deprovisioning, percentage of user accounts mapped to approved roles/entitlements, and the rate and count of overdue or exception access review findings to demonstrate access to information is managed and unauthorized access is prevented.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control focused on coverage and correctness (best expressed as percentages of accounts/entitlements in compliance). Counts (e.g., orphaned accounts, overdue reviews) and trends (improvement over review cycles) supplement percentage coverage metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of active accounts that are assigned to an approved role or entitlement catalog, percent of provisioning/deprovisioning actions completed within SLA, count of orphaned/unused accounts and privileged accounts lacking required controls, and count/percentage of scheduled access reviews completed on time vs overdue. Include timestamps for requests, approvals, and fulfillment to allow SLA calculation and MTTx queries.
data_signals: user account inventory with role/entitlement and active/inactive status
access provisioning request records with request, approval and fulfillment timestamps
access deprovisioning/revocation timestamps for terminated or role-changed users
access review schedule records and completion evidence (reviewer, date, outcome)
privileged account inventory (privilege level) and MFA enforcement status
orphaned/unused account detection (last authentication timestamp)
remote access/VPN session logs indicating authenticated sessions from remote work sites
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated identity management logs with request/approval/fulfillment timestamps and role mappings allowing SLA and percent-compliance calculations; continuous synchronization with authoritative HR/identity source.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-time screenshots of user lists without timestamps or approval trail; manual attestations without machine-readable evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title and associated risks emphasize preventing unauthorized access and managing access lifecycle (provisioning/deprovisioning), which are preventive in nature.

IAC-31.differentiation_note: Unlike logging or incident controls, this entry focuses specifically on provisioning/deprovisioning, role/entitlement alignment, and access review completion metrics (coverage and timeliness) rather than detection or remediation metrics.

IAC-32.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of access attempts and active identities that are authenticated using approved methods (per policy), and count unauthorized or failed authentication attempts and unprotected service/entity accounts to demonstrate authentication is enforced for persons and entities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Authentication controls are primarily preventive (coverage/compliance) so percent coverage (e.g., percent of systems enforcing approved auth methods or percent of accounts with MFA) is most useful; counts and rates (failed auths, unauthorized attempts) supplement to show event volume and detection.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of systems/users/service-entities that use approved authentication methods (per policy), count of authentication requests by result (success, failure, rejected-unauthed), and count of active identities or service accounts without enforced authentication. Group metrics by system, identity type (human vs service), and remote/local access.
data_signals: authentication event logs (timestamp, principal id, auth method, result)
MFA/enrollment records per user/principal
SSO/OAuth token issuance logs and token type
service account and entity inventory with authentication configuration
failed authentication attempt logs and rejection reasons
unauthorized access attempt alerts or blocked access logs
remote access authentication logs (VPN/VDI) indicating source and result
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, timestamped authentication logs correlated with identity directory and MFA status showing enforcement across systems; automated enforcement records (SSO/MFA enforced) per system.
Weak: point-in-time screenshots or periodic attestations without correlated logs; manual lists of accounts without logs of authentication events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title requires that persons/entities are authenticated (a preventative access enforcement requirement) rather than solely detecting incidents.

IAC-32.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on proving authentication enforcement for persons and machine/entities (coverage and auth-event evidence), rather than downstream access-review, session monitoring, or incident response measurements.

IAC-33.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and correctness of unique identifiers assigned to user accounts: count of active accounts with a company-assigned unique name/number, count of accounts lacking or sharing identifiers, and number of authentication events that cannot be attributed to a unique user identifier.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective control is best evidenced by counts (e.g., number of accounts without unique IDs, number of duplicate/shared IDs). Percentages/ rates are useful secondary metrics to express coverage and trends over time (coverage % of accounts with unique identifiers, rate of new duplicate ID events).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count active user accounts in the authoritative identity store and the subset that have an assigned unique name or number; count of duplicate or shared identifiers; count of authentication events that map to an unknown or non-unique identifier. Also measure provisioning events that assign identifiers and any exception tickets created for identifier conflicts.
data_signals: user account records containing name and/or number fields from the authoritative identity provider
identity provider (IdP) provisioning/create/update event logs showing assignment of name/number
authentication event logs with principal/subject field and timestamp
duplicate identifier detection outputs (lists of identical name/number values across accounts)
mapping table of account identifier → employee/person entity (account ownership)
exceptions or helpdesk tickets for shared/generic accounts
audit logs linking user identifier to performed actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative IdP records and provisioning logs showing a unique name/number for every active account plus authentication logs mapping each event to that unique identifier.
Strong: automated duplicate-detection reports with remediation tickets closed.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-time screenshots of user lists without timestamps or provisioning history.
Weak: audit evidence that lacks authentication-event linkage (i.e., identifiers present in directory but not shown in access logs)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text emphasizes assigning identifiers for 'identifying and tracking user identity', which supports detection and attribution rather than prevention.

IAC-33.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of unique identifier assignment and traceability of actions to those identifiers (coverage and duplicate/shared-ID detection), rather than measuring access enforcement policies or authentication strength; focus is on uniqueness and auditability.

IAC-34.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of workforce members who have completed required clearance procedures (e.g., background checks, signed confidentiality agreements) prior to receiving system access, and count instances where access was provisioned without completed clearance or with an approved waiver.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage (what percent of workforce are cleared) as the primary assurance; counts of exceptions and rates/trends of clearance completion are useful secondary signals to demonstrate ongoing compliance and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the proportion of active workforce accounts where clearance artifacts are present and dated prior to first access provisioning. Also measure the count of accounts with access provisioned before clearance completion, number of approved waivers, and time delta between hire/provisioning and clearance completion.
data_signals: hire/onboarding date from HR system
background-check / clearance completion timestamp (and result) from HR/vendor records
signed confidentiality/non-disclosure agreement timestamps
access provisioning events (account creation, role assignment) with timestamps and approver ID
waiver/exception approval records and timestamps
active account list and mapping to employee IDs
termination date or access deprovisioning timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR-to-IAM integration showing clearance completion timestamp prior to provisioning and system-enforced blocking of provisioning until clearance exists.
Strong: auditable waiver records with approver and expiry for any exceptions.
Weak: manual spreadsheets, unsigned scan snapshots, or undated PDF clearance files without linkage to provisioning events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name requires clearance procedures to be implemented to prevent unauthorized access, indicating a preventive control that pre-approves workforce eligibility before access is granted.

IAC-34.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on personnel vetting/clearance and timing of provisioning (people-process control), not on technical access enforcement or logging — measurement must link HR clearance artifacts to IAM provisioning events.

IAC-35.measurement_goal: Measure workforce security by reporting coverage and timeliness of account provisioning/deprovisioning, percentage of workforce with role-assigned security responsibilities, training completion rate, and endpoint/compliance posture for remote workers to demonstrate prevention of unauthorized access and insecure remote work.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Workforce security is primarily about coverage and compliance (percent of workforce meeting requirements), with counts/rates for event volumes (e.g., overdue deprovisions) and trends to show ongoing improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete measures an auditor would want: (1) percentage of terminated or role-changed employees whose accounts were disabled/updated within the policy SLA (e.g., 24 hours) using HR termination timestamps correlated to account disable timestamps; (2) percentage of active workforce with completed security awareness training within the required period; (3) percentage of remote/endpoint devices that meet baseline security posture (MFA enabled, device management agent present); (4) count and percentage of privileged accounts with documented owners and assigned responsibilities.
data_signals: HR employee termination and role-change timestamps
user account provisioning/disable/modify event timestamps from identity management system
access review records and completion timestamps
LMS training completion records and training due dates
privileged account register (account id, owner, assigned responsibilities)
endpoint compliance scan results (MFA enabled flag, device management agent present, OS patch status)
remote access logs indicating managed vs unmanaged device connection
documented role/responsibility assignment records (policy sign-offs, job descriptions)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated identity management logs correlated with HR system timestamps showing timely disable/provisioning and continuous endpoint compliance scan results; LMS records with user IDs and timestamps.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off attestations of workforce security, annual-only training rosters, or single point-in-time screenshots that do not show timeliness or coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Workforce security implemented' and associated risks (unauthorized breach, insecure remote work, unclear responsibilities) indicate measures that prevent incidents by restricting access and enforcing workforce policies.

IAC-35.differentiation_note: This control focuses on workforce lifecycle, role/responsibility assignment, training and remote endpoint posture (people/process coverage) rather than purely technical detection or vulnerability scanning — measurement must correlate HR, identity, training and endpoint signals.

IRO-12.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of 'additional breach information' as captured in incident/breach reports and associated administrative artifacts (contact lists, cyber insurance records, forensic evidence). Specifically, report the rate of incidents whose reports include all required additional fields and the time to update contact/insurance/forensic records after detection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about detecting whether required additional breach information is present in incident reports (a proportion/rate of compliant reports). Percentage/count support numerator/denominator and trend shows improvement in reporting completeness and timeliness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the proportion of breach/incident records that contain all required 'additional breach information' fields (e.g., affected data types, estimated affected individuals/records, detection and notification timestamps, designated reporting contact, cyber insurance policy reference, forensic evidence collected) and measure time intervals: detection→reporting and detection→contact-list/insurance record update. Also count incidents missing one or more required fields and track chain-of-custody / forensic artifact logs for each incident.
data_signals: incident/breach report records containing fields: affected_data_types, estimated_affected_count, detection_timestamp, notification_timestamp, reporting_contact_id
incident report completeness flags or validation errors for required additional fields
incident contact list entries with last_updated_timestamp and owner
cyber insurance policy record presence, policy_id, coverage_effective_date and expiry_date
forensic evidence collection logs and chain_of_custody entries per incident (timestamps, collector_id, evidence_id)
root cause analysis (RCA) record completion timestamps and remediation tracking items linked to the incident
count of incidents missing required additional information by incident severity/type
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident records with required structured fields populated, timestamped updates to contact lists and insurance records, and complete chain-of-custody forensic logs.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads, one-off spreadsheets, or retrospective narrative reports without structured fields or timestamps.
Strong: demonstrable time intervals computed from detection_timestamp to notification_timestamp and to contact/insurance updates; Weak: absence of timestamps or manual estimates.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on capturing and reporting additional breach information, which is primarily about detecting/recording and documenting incidents rather than preventing them.

IRO-12.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets completeness and timeliness of breach reporting artifacts (contact lists, insurance records, forensic logs) rather than detection of incidents or remediation of vulnerabilities; measurement must focus on structured report fields and administrative record currency. If no perfect focus_area fits all administrative aspects, incident_response was chosen as closest match.

IRO-13.measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and completeness of identifying individuals affected by a breach: percentage and count of security incidents where affected individuals were correctly identified and contactable within the defined notification timeframe, plus mean time-to-identify per incident.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is about identifying affected individuals per incident (a detection/response activity) so measuring the rate of incidents with identification completed within target time is primary; percentage (coverage/completeness), raw counts (number of affected individuals/incidents), and trends (timeliness over time) provide useful context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each security incident, capture the incident ID, detection timestamp, identification_complete timestamp, the set/count of affected individual identifiers discovered, and whether contact information for each identified individual exists and was validated. Aggregate: percent of incidents with complete identification within the notification SLA, mean time-to-identify, and count of incidents with missing/invalid contact info.
data_signals: incident ticket ID with detection_time and identification_complete_time
list of affected individual identifiers per incident (e.g., patient_id, account_id, record_id)
notification dispatch logs (recipient identifier, contact_method, send_timestamp, delivery_status)
contact registry / appropriate contacts dataset (name, role, email, phone, last_verified_date)
forensic mapping artifacts linking compromised records to individual IDs (extracted record identifiers, file/table/row references)
count of incidents flagged as 'unable to identify individuals' or 'missing contact info'
regulatory/notice filing timestamps (e.g., breach notice submission timestamp) correlated to identification completion
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident records with timestamps showing identification_complete within SLA, consistent mapping from forensic artifacts to individual IDs, and delivery-statused notification logs
Weak: ad-hoc emails or manual spreadsheets without timestamps or forensic mapping, or incidents lacking verifiable contact registry entries

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on identifying affected individuals after a breach (a detection/assessment activity), so it is primarily detective in nature.

IRO-13.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about mapping breaches to individual persons and proving timely notification—unlike sibling IR controls focused on containment or forensic capability, it requires metrics about individual identifiers, contact registry completeness, and time-to-identify.

IRO-14.measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of security incidents discovered by the company that were reported to the group health plan, and the timeliness/completeness of those reports (discovery-to-report latency and presence of required report artifacts).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This corrective control is about whether incidents that occur are actually reported and how quickly — a rate (e.g., incidents reported within required timeframe per total incidents) best shows operational compliance; percentages and raw counts supplement for coverage and volume.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of security incidents discovered by the company, count of those incidents for which a report to the group health plan was created/sent, discovery_timestamp and report_timestamp for each incident, record of report content and any acknowledgement from the group health plan, and an indicator/flag that the incident met reporting criteria.
data_signals: incident_id
incident_discovery_timestamp
incident_report_sent_timestamp
report_recipient (group health plan) and contact details
stored incident report artifact (email, portal submission, PDF) with content hash
acknowledgement_receipt or confirmation from group health plan
incident_classification/reportable_flag (was incident required to be reported)
incident_severity and remediation_status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident management records showing discovery_timestamp, report_sent_timestamp, stored report artifact and acknowledgement from the group health plan for every incident.
Strong: full coverage where every incident record has a corresponding report record and acknowledgement.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads without consistent timestamps, manual notes, or retrospective attestations that cannot be mapped to incident IDs.
Weak: evidence limited to summary reports or periodic spreadsheets without raw artifact or acknowledgement linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires reporting after the company becomes aware of a security incident — a reactive, post-incident action (reporting) consistent with corrective classification.

IRO-14.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires external reporting to the group health plan (timeliness and completeness of notification) rather than internal detection, forensic capability, or remediation metrics that sibling incident response controls would measure.

IRO-15.measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and completeness of breach notifications: percentage and count of notifiable breaches for which required internal and external notifications were sent within the organization's policy timeframe, and verification that contact lists and notification evidence (delivery receipts, regulatory filings) exist for each breach.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Notification effectiveness is best expressed as a rate (timely-notification rate) and complemented by percentage on-time and counts of notifications; these measure timeliness and coverage of the corrective/response activity described by the control.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each breach event record: breach incident ID, detection timestamp, notification sent timestamp(s) to required parties (regulators, affected individuals, internal escalation), notification delivery evidence (email delivery receipt or postal tracking), and contact list version/timestamp used. Aggregate into a rate of notifications completed within policy timeframes and counts of overdue or missing notifications.
data_signals: breach incident identifier (incident ID)
breach detection timestamp / incident discovery time
notification sent timestamp(s) to regulator(s) and affected individuals
notification delivery receipts or mail tracking numbers
contact list last-updated timestamp and contact owner metadata
regulatory filing confirmation (filing ID or acknowledgment)
forensic engagement initiation timestamp and forensic report IDs
cyber insurance policy record (policy number, effective/expiration dates)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident ticket records with detection and notification timestamps, delivery receipts, and signed regulatory filing acknowledgements.
Strong: an auditable contact directory with last-updated timestamps and owner approvals tied to each notification.
Weak: undated or manually summarized notification descriptions, verbal confirmations, or one-off post-incident slides without raw timestamps.
Weak: absence of delivery receipts or failure to show which contact list version was used for notification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control ('Notification of breach') is an incident-response activity performed after a breach is detected, hence corrective in nature.

IRO-15.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of notification timeliness, delivery evidence, and contact-list currency — unlike other IRO controls that may focus on detection, forensics, or remediation metrics.

IRO-16.measurement_goal: Measure the number and classification of security incidents detected and the timeliness and completeness of reporting (internal escalation, regulatory/affected-party notification) and forensic evidence preservation; e.g., count of incidents per period, mean time from detection to report, percentage of incidents reported within policy timelines, and percentage of incidents with preserved chain-of-custody and completed root-cause analysis.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a detective/control-operations measure where auditors want counts of incidents and events; rates (e.g., detection/reporting rates) and percentages (timely reporting, preservation coverage) complement counts to show effectiveness and coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of security incidents detected per defined period by detection source and classification; time intervals for detection→internal report and detection→regulatory/affected-party notification; percentage of incidents with documented forensic preservation/chain-of-custody and completed root-cause analysis within policy timelines. Include the presence of cyber insurance documentation where relevant to associated risk mitigation.
data_signals: incident record entries with detection_timestamp, reporter_id, incident_classification (breach/security event/near-miss)
SIEM/IDS/endpoint alert IDs correlated to incident IDs with source and priority
detection_to_report interval (detection_timestamp → internal_report_timestamp) per incident
regulatory/affected-party notification logs with send_timestamp and recipient list
chain-of-custody / forensic preservation logs and timestamps (evidence_collected_timestamp, custodian_id)
incident response playbook invocation flag and playbook_version referenced in incident record
root cause analysis reports and remediation action items with status and completion timestamps
cyber insurance policy file / policy_number (presence/coverage) — from associated risk documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated SIEM/alert-to-incident correlation logs with immutable timestamps, documented internal report timestamps, preserved chain-of-custody entries, and timely regulatory notifications.
Strong: forensics artifacts and completed root-cause reports linked to incident IDs showing remediation and lessons learned.
Weak: single post-incident narrative summaries, ad-hoc emails without timestamps, or tabletop-only exercise notes that do not show actual detection/reporting events.
Weak: incident counts without correlated detection timestamps or missing evidence of notification or forensic preservation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Security incidents identified and reported' focuses on detection and reporting activities, which are characteristic of detective controls.

IRO-16.differentiation_note: This control is focused on detection, timeliness of reporting, and forensic evidence capture (identification/reporting metrics), rather than on remediation or prevention metrics — measurement must emphasize event counts, detection→report durations, notification logs, and chain-of-custody artifacts.

IRO-17.measurement_goal: Show that approved incident response policies and procedures exist, are reviewed, and are actively applied to incidents by measuring: (1) percent of incidents handled with a documented post-incident report and root-cause analysis within the policy timeframe, and (2) mean time to contain and mean time to recover per incident along with counts of incidents where procedures were not followed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a corrective control, the most important metrics are response/remediation rates (e.g., MTTR, percent of incidents remediated on time). Counts of incidents and percentages of procedure adherence are useful secondary measures to show coverage and compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Measure incident lifecycle timestamps (detection, acknowledgement, containment, eradication, recovery), presence and timestamp of post-incident report and root-cause analysis, assignment of incident response roles, number of incidents flagged as non-compliant with documented procedures, and completion/closure timestamps for remediation tasks mapped to the incident.
data_signals: incident response policy document version and approval/last-review date
incident ticket records with timestamps for detection, containment, eradication, recovery
post-incident report existence and timestamp (with root-cause analysis field)
forensic evidence collection logs and chain-of-custody entries
assigned incident handler IDs/role assignments on incident tickets
remediation task IDs and closure timestamps linked to incident
tabletop exercise or IR exercise logs and attendance records
incident SLA flags / overdue remediation indicators
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident ticketing records with full timestamped lifecycle fields, linked post-incident reports, and forensic chain-of-custody logs; regular review entries showing policy versions and review dates.

Weak: one-off or manual incident summaries, or annual policy documents without evidence of application to specific incidents; absence of timestamps or missing linkage between remediation tasks and incidents.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text requires procedures to address security incidents (response and remediation), which is corrective in nature because it focuses on actions taken after incidents occur.

IRO-17.differentiation_note: This control is policy/procedure-focused and requires evidence both of documented incident response procedures (governance) and their operational application (ticket timestamps, post-incident reports), unlike sibling controls that might emphasize detection coverage or vulnerability scanning; measurement must therefore combine documentation presence with applied incident-level metrics.

IRO-18.measurement_goal: Measure the time between incident detection and formal breach notification (to regulators, affected individuals, and insurer) and the percentage/count of notifications delivered within jurisdictional/regulatory SLAs; also measure currency of notification contact lists used during incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is an incident-response performance control focused on timeliness (time-to-notify and SLA adherence). 'Rate' best captures notification latency metrics (e.g., notifications per SLA window); percentages measure SLA compliance and counts show volume of incidents/late notifications.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each incident, capture the detection timestamp and the timestamps of each formal notification (regulator, affected parties, cyber insurer). Calculate time-to-notify (hours) per recipient and compute the percent of notifications meeting the SLA by jurisdiction per reporting period. Also capture the notification contact list last-updated timestamp and evidence that the list used in the incident matched the authoritative contact registry.
data_signals: incident detection timestamp (system or SOC alert time)
notification sent timestamp to regulator(s)
notification sent timestamp to affected individual(s)
notification sent timestamp to cyber insurer (if applicable)
jurisdictional/regulatory notification SLA or deadline per incident
authoritative notification contact list version and last_updated timestamp
receipt/acknowledgement logs or delivery status for sent notifications
record of fines or regulatory enforcement actions linked to notification timing
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped notification logs and delivery receipts correlated to incident IDs and jurisdictional SLA definitions
Strong: contact list records showing last update before incident plus audit trail proving the list was referenced during notification
Weak: undated email threads or oral recollections of notification timing without system timestamps
Weak: policy text alone or one-off manual attestations without cross-linked incident timestamps

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control measures the speed and effectiveness of incident response activities (notification timing and reporting) — corrective/response behavior rather than preventive detection.

IRO-18.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on notification latency and contact-list currency (time-to-notify, SLA compliance, insurer/regulator notification) versus other incident controls that measure forensic capability, root-cause remediation, or detection coverage.

MNT-2.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of maintenance records for assets and systems: percentage of assets with an up-to-date maintenance record that includes assigned owner/contact and last update timestamp, plus count of overdue or missing maintenance records against the policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control about records coverage and currency, so a coverage percentage (e.g., percent of assets with current records) is most useful; counts of missing/overdue records and trends over time are useful secondary metrics to show remediation and currency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of assets/systems in the authoritative inventory and the subset that have a maintenance record with: assigned owner/contact details, last_updated timestamp, record of last maintenance activity, and scheduled next maintenance or retention metadata. Calculate the percent of assets with current records (updated within policy timeframe) and list of assets with missing or overdue records.
data_signals: asset_id mapped against maintenance_record_id (authoritative inventory linkage)
maintenance_record.last_updated_timestamp
maintenance_record.assigned_owner_contact (name/email/phone)
maintenance_record.last_maintenance_activity_description and completion_ticket_id
scheduled_next_maintenance_date or maintenance_frequency policy field
record_retention_label or retention_expiry_date
audit_log entries showing maintenance_record creation/modification with actor and timestamp
open/overdue_maintenance_record_count (records beyond scheduled/expected update)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated authoritative inventory/CMDB records with timestamps and audit trail showing recent updates and contact fields populated; cross-referenced maintenance tickets for completion.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off attachments without timestamps, missing owner contact details, or records that are not linked to the asset inventory.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The requirement that maintenance records be 'maintained' is proactive — ensuring records and contacts exist to prevent delays in response and unclear responsibilities.

MNT-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of records completeness and currency (owner/contact, timestamps, retention) rather than measurements of the maintenance activity quality or remediation speed — its unique need is verifying up-to-date, linked maintenance records for assets.

MON-8.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, coverage, and operational effectiveness of implemented audit controls: percent of in-scope systems with audit/logging enabled and forwarded to a central collector, retention/configuration compliance against policy, and volume/timeliness of reviewed audit events that lead to alerts or findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Audit controls are primarily detective — counts of enabled log sources, generated audit events, and alerts provide direct evidence; percentages (coverage/retention compliance) and trends (event volumes, review frequency) supplement to show coverage and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of in-scope systems (and assets handling PHI) with audit/logging enabled and actively forwarding logs to the central collector; percentage of log sources meeting retention and event-type coverage policy; count and timestamps of audit log reviews and resulting alerts/incidents, and presence of assigned owners and project artifacts showing audit controls were implemented.
data_signals: audit logging enabled flag per host/service (system-level configuration records)
log forwarding/ingestion status to central SIEM (last successful ingest timestamp)
log retention period configuration per log source
list of captured event types (authentication, access, configuration change) per source
count of audit events generated per source/time window
audit log review records (reviewer ID, review timestamp, findings)
alerts/incidents created from audit events (alert timestamp, source, disposition)
mapping of in-scope/PHI-handling systems to audit coverage (asset inventory linkage)
project plan or change request evidence showing audit controls implemented
assigned owner/role for audit control operation and review
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated ingestion of logs into a central, immutable store with timestamps and configuration metadata; regular, recorded audit-log reviews with reviewer identity and findings; documented mapping of all in-scope systems to log sources.
Weak: ad-hoc screenshots or one-off exported logs, partial/manual log collection, missing retention/configuration metadata, or absence of reviewer records and ownership assignments.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Audit controls implemented' implies monitoring and logging to detect events; audit controls primarily provide detective evidence of security activity and incidents.

MON-8.differentiation_note: This control focuses on implementation and coverage of audit/logging controls (enablement, forwarding, retention, and review) rather than remediation, access configuration, or project governance — measurement must target log-source coverage and review artifacts rather than incident handling metrics.

MON-9.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage of automated integrity controls on systems and datasets holding protected health information (PHI) (percentage of assets with integrity checks enabled), the count and rate of integrity violations detected over time, and the time-to-detect and time-to-remediate detected integrity failures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Preventive verification of integrity is best shown by coverage/percentage metrics (what percent of critical datasets/systems have integrity controls active). Counts and rates capture detected integrity events and trends for residual risk and remediation performance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of critical/PHI-bearing systems and datasets with automated integrity controls enabled (e.g., file-integrity monitoring, checksums/hashing, database change auditing, backup verification). 2) Count and timestamps of integrity violation alerts (unauthorized or unexpected modifications) grouped by severity and system. 3) Mean time to detect and mean time to remediate integrity incidents and number of records restored or reconciled.
data_signals: file integrity monitoring (FIM) alert records with timestamp, affected file/object, detected change type and actor
checksum/hash verification logs showing baseline hash and current hash and verification timestamp
database transaction audit logs (transaction id, user, operation, timestamp) for PHI tables
backup integrity and verification logs (backup id, verification pass/fail, timestamp)
system/config inventory indicating integrity-control enabled status per asset (FIM enabled boolean, agent version)
integrity incident case records with detection timestamp, remediation start/end timestamps, and remediation outcome
immutable/append-only audit log storage evidence (WORM or tamper-evidence) and retention metadata
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated FIM/checksum verification logs and tamper-evident audit storage with timestamps and asset mapping; integrated incident records with remediation timestamps.
Moderate: regular scheduled verification reports and backup verification logs without real-time alerts.
Weak: ad-hoc manual checks, one-off attestations, or infrequent manual reconciliation with no audit trail or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrasing 'Data integrity maintained' indicates preventive controls (integrity checks, verification) intended to keep data unaltered rather than only detect after the fact.

MON-9.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about verifying and maintaining data integrity (coverage of integrity controls and detection/remediation metrics) rather than the related operational risks like contacts, project planning, or remote-work security; measurement must focus on integrity verification logs, checksums, backups and remediation timelines.

MON-10.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of authentication mechanisms protecting ePHI: percentage of ePHI-containing systems and accounts enforcing required authentication (e.g., MFA/password policy) and trends in authentication events (success/failure) for ePHI access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on implementation/coverage (best expressed as percent of ePHI assets/users with enforced authentication). Counts of authenticated systems/accounts and rates of authentication failures are useful secondary indicators of operation and risk.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of assets and user accounts that access or store ePHI that have an enforced authentication mechanism configured (MFA enabled or configured password policy) and track authentication event counts and failure rates for those assets, correlated by asset/user and remote access origin. Also measure time-to-enable authentication for newly onboarded ePHI systems.
data_signals: inventory of systems storing or processing ePHI with attribute: authentication_required/authentication_enabled
list of user accounts authorized to access ePHI with MFA_enrollment flag and authentication_method
authentication event logs for ePHI systems (userID, timestamp, success/failure, auth_method, source IP)
authentication configuration records (MFA enabled, password complexity policy, SSO/SAML/OAuth config) per system
VPN/remote-access connection logs to ePHI systems (timestamp, user, success/failure)
onboarding/config change audit records showing when authentication was enabled for an ePHI system
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated records showing authentication configuration flags and real-time auth event logs correlated to a verified ePHI asset inventory (demonstrates enforcement and operation).
Strong: MFA enrollment lists and per-system config snapshots exported from IAM/SSO systems.
Weak: one-time attestations or screenshots of an authentication setting without correlated logs or asset mapping.
Weak: samples of user accounts without mapping to the full ePHI asset inventory (doesn't prove coverage).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires an authentication mechanism to be implemented for ePHI, which prevents unauthorized access rather than detecting or correcting incidents.

MON-10.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets implementation and coverage of authentication for ePHI assets/accounts (coverage and enforcement metrics), unlike sibling controls that might focus on logging, incident response, or general access reviews.

PES-16.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and enforcement of access control and validation procedures across systems, physical locations and remote work sites: percent of systems and entry points with implemented access control procedures; rate and count of access provisioning/revocation actions that include required validation evidence; and count/rate of unauthorized access attempts detected and blocked.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (implementation/coverage) so percentage metrics (coverage, compliance %) best show how much of the environment is protected; counts and rates capture provisioning/unauthorized attempt volumes and trends for operational validation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of systems, applications and physical entry points that have documented access control and validation procedures implemented and attested. 2) Count and timestamps of provisioning/revocation actions including approver ID and validation artifacts. 3) Count and rate of unauthorized/failed access attempts and blocked accesses by system and location. 4) Presence of remote access validation records (VPN/device posture) for remote work sites.
data_signals: access control policy document versions and implementation attestation records
user provisioning and deprovisioning logs with approver IDs and validation evidence
access review completion records and attestation timestamps
authentication/authorization logs including failed and blocked access attempts by user and system
remote access/VPN connection logs and device posture/validation results for remote work sites
system and physical asset inventory mapped to implemented access control procedures
incident records of breaches or unauthorized access related to access controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, time-stamped provisioning logs, continuous auth/access logs, complete system/physical inventory showing implemented procedures, and periodic attestation records demonstrating validation.
Weak: single point-in-time manual attestations, missing provisioning approver IDs, incomplete inventory, or absence of access/remote validation logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title requires 'access control and validation procedures implemented', indicating preventive implementation and validation of controls to stop unauthorized access.

PES-16.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring both logical (user/provisioning/auth logs, remote access validation) and physical (entry point/procedural) access coverage and validation, unlike sibling controls that may target only logical access or only physical/environmental resiliency.

PES-17.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of facilities and controlled entry points covered by implemented physical access controls (electronic badge readers, locks, visitor procedures), plus counts/rates of unauthorized access events and environmental/equipment maintenance events that could affect availability.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage across facilities (best expressed as percentage coverage). Counts and rates are useful secondary measures for tracking incidents (unauthorized access, door-alarm events) and trends over time for availability-related signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of facilities and of defined controlled entry points that have active electronic access controls (badge readers, locks) and documented visitor-control procedures. 2) Count/rate of unauthorized access events (badge-denied, forced-open, tailgating reports) and counts of environmental/equipment incidents (UPS/generator test failures, temperature/water alarms) for critical areas. 3) Retention/availability of CCTV recordings and access-log retention compliance compared to policy.
data_signals: facility inventory with controlled entry points and control type (badge reader / lock / manned)
badge access event logs (door_id, badge_id, timestamp, access_result)
door forced-open/held-open alarm logs (door_id, timestamp, alarm_type)
visitor sign-in logs and escort/host approvals (visitor_id, timestamp, host)
CCTV camera inventory and recording retention logs (camera_id, coverage_area, retention_period, last_recording_timestamp)
environmental sensor logs for critical rooms (temperature, humidity, water-leak events) with timestamps
UPS/generator maintenance and test records with dates and results
exceptions/approved access lists and last access-review timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Centralized, continuous electronic badge logs and alarm feeds covering all controlled entry points plus automated CCTV retention reports and recent maintenance/test results.
Moderate: Regularly collected visitor logs and periodic physical inspection checklists that are reconciled against inventory.
Weak: Policy documents alone, occasional ad-hoc inspection notes, or manual sign-in sheets without timestamps or retention proof.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Facility access controls implemented' describes measures to prevent unauthorized physical access and protect availability, so it is primarily preventive.

PES-17.differentiation_note: This control focuses on physical facility and environmental/equipment protections (badge readers, CCTV, environmental sensors, maintenance) rather than logical or network access controls; measurement must emphasize physical entry-point coverage and environmental availability signals.

PES-18.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that a documented facility security plan exists, is approved and reviewed on schedule, and that required physical and environmental controls from the plan (access controls, environmental controls, equipment redundancy and maintenance, assigned responsibilities) are implemented and functioning — measured as percent of required controls implemented and counts of failed/overdue maintenance or incident events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (plan and implementation coverage) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance) show how much of the plan is in effect; counts and trends help track incidents, maintenance failures, and remediation velocity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and timestamped evidence of the facility security plan (document version, approval date, next review date); percentage of plan-defined physical controls implemented per site; counts of completed vs overdue maintenance/tests for critical equipment (UPS/generators, HVAC, fire suppression); counts of physical security incidents and badge/entry anomalies tied to facilities; records assigning information-security responsibilities for facility operations.
data_signals: facility security plan document metadata (version, approval timestamp, next review date)
site-by-site control implementation checklist / coverage matrix (control present = yes/no)
physical access control logs and badge access lists (events by location and time)
incident and breach reports referencing facility access or environmental events
environmental monitoring logs and test records (temperature/humidity/fire suppression test timestamps)
UPS/generator maintenance and test records and failure tickets
inspection checklists and remediation ticket counts (completed vs overdue)
records of assigned information security responsibilities for facility operations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: version-controlled plan document with approval signature and scheduled review records plus automated environmental and access-control logs correlated to site checklists and maintenance records.
Strong: continuous monitoring and automated maintenance test logs showing successful tests and low overdue counts.
Weak: single point-in-time checklist or an unsigned plan document, manual ad-hoc emails describing controls, or annual-only test reports without regular maintenance evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires a 'facility security plan implemented' which is intended to prevent breaches, equipment failures, and unavailability by establishing controls and responsibilities.

PES-18.differentiation_note: Unlike procedural or incident-focused siblings, PES-18 specifically requires measurement of a documented facility plan plus operationalization of physical/environmental controls (implementation coverage, maintenance/test records and assigned responsibilities); no single technical detection metric suffices, so measures must combine document-controls coverage with physical operations data. Note: physical-security focus_area not in valid list so closest applicable focus_areas were chosen.

RSK-8.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, frequency and coverage of formal reviews of information system activity: count of completed review events per system per period, percentage of critical systems reviewed on schedule, and count/severity of findings discovered and tracked to remediation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is detective in nature (reviews produce discrete events/findings) so counts of review events and findings are most useful; percentages (coverage on-schedule) and trending over time show improvement or lapses against policy.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of information system activity review records per system and per review period (including timestamp and reviewer), mapping of reviewed systems to the authoritative asset inventory and criticality tier, count and severity of findings logged from reviews, and remediation ticket status/timestamps for findings. Also measure schedule adherence (reviews performed vs. scheduled) and number of missed or overdue reviews.
data_signals: information system activity review records (review ID, timestamp, reviewer, scope)
review schedule/calendar entries and recurrence rules
asset inventory entries mapped to reviewed systems (asset ID, criticality tier)
review findings log (finding ID, severity, description, evidence reference)
remediation/ticketing records linked to review findings (ticket ID, created/resolved timestamps, resolution status)
missed/overdue review exceptions and documented approvals
remote access or remote work site references in review scope (evidence of remote site review)
policy reference for required review frequency and reviewer role
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped review records and logs with reviewer identity, explicit mapping to asset inventory and linked remediation tickets showing closure.
Strong: recurring scheduled reviews with zero missed occurrences for critical systems over the audit period.
Weak: ad-hoc attestations or one-off screenshots without timestamps, scope, or linkage to asset inventory.
Weak: manual narrative statements of reviews without linked findings or remediation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on reviewing system activity (a monitoring/detection function) so it detects issues via review events and findings.

RSK-8.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurable evidence of periodic review activity and findings tracking (review records, coverage vs. asset inventory, and remediation linkage), rather than policy creation, role assignment, or secure configuration which would require different coverage and compliance metrics.

RSK-9.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of active projects and asset groups that have completed and approved security risk assessments (including mobile and remote-work considerations), the count of identified risks per project, and the percentage of those risks with assigned owners and documented treatment actions tracked to closure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about coverage and completeness of risk analysis (a preventive activity), so percentage metrics (coverage of projects/assets assessed, percent of risks with treatment/owners) are primary; counts (number of risks, unassessed projects), trends (coverage over time), and risk-scores supplement to show progress and severity distribution.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active projects with an approved risk assessment prior to project milestone or deployment; count of risk register entries created per project including severity and owner; percent of identified risks that have an assigned owner and documented treatment plan with target remediation dates; count of mobile-device and remote-work risk assessments completed versus inventory of mobile/remote exposures.
data_signals: project inventory with risk_assessment_completed flag and assessment timestamp
risk register entries (risk_id, project_id, description, severity/score, created_timestamp)
risk owner assignment fields (owner_id, assignment_timestamp)
treatment plan records (mitigation actions, target_completion_date, status)
risk assessment approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp)
mobile device risk assessment documents/records (assessment_id, scope, findings)
